title: Take the Kids on Your Next Wine Trip. Seriously.
summary:
As wineries rethink their approach to hospitality, wine country is becoming the family destination you never expected.

The post Take the Kids on Your Next Wine Trip. Seriously. appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
This year, my 21-year-old son and I went to our first wine festival together: the Rhode Island Wine Experience. Granted, he’s old enough now to understand the basics of wine regions and flavor profiles, but as we tasted from winemakers around the world, we got plenty of comments about how cool it was for a mother and son to bond over wine.

“How did you get him so interested?” people asked me in amazement.

My family’s love for wine goes way back. I’ve been taking my three kids (and nieces and nephews) on vineyard trips for years. Until recently, wine country was largely seen as a sophisticated, adult-only escape, but today many wineries have begun opening their doors to children with play areas, access to farm animals, and casual food options. After all, little ones bring parents, and that means more families can embrace wine country travel without feeling left out.  

Given that the wine industry has spent the past few years grappling with ways to include more people, this shift in attitude makes sense.

In the United States, per-capita wine consumption has been falling since its 2017 peak, according to IWSR, with Gen Z drinking less alcohol than generations preceding them. American tasting rooms, in particular, have suffered from dwindling interest. Against that backdrop, the calculus of welcoming families to wineries has shifted into a new style of hospitality.

‘Return to what wine country has always been’ 

At some estates, the presence of children is organic. “We don’t see it as a long-term strategy so much as a return to what wine country has always been,” says Dan Zepponi, president and CEO of Cuvaison in Napa Valley. The property, which features two open-air boathouse pavilions overlooking the winery’s pond, also includes The VineYard, a family-friendly outdoor zone designed for relaxed, self-paced tastings, with lots of room to move and vineyard views.

“When people experience wine as part of time spent together, across generations, around food, and near nature, it feels authentic and special,” says Zepponi.  Rather than emphasizing transactions (i.e. bottle sales), these wineries are leaning into a memory-making model.

At Demetria Estate in the Santa Ynez Valley, catering to families just made sense. The biodynamic property features sweeping lawns, resident dogs, and horseback riding through the hills above Santa Barbara wine country. Owner Alexis Zahoudanis says hospitality runs through her veins.  

“Demetria has always been a family-owned and operated estate, rooted in my father’s Greek heritage and my mother’s Southern hospitality,” she says. “When they founded Demetria two decades ago, their vision was to create a place where guests could relax, connect with nature, and experience an authentic, lived-in wine estate.” She says the family-friendly activities evolved organically as guests responded favorably to that sense of home. 

The result is a destination where a serious wine tasting and a child’s afternoon of adventure can coexist. Parents are unhurried because their kids are genuinely occupied, and the wine still gets the attention it deserves.

That dynamic is something Karen DeGolia, proprietor of Limerick Lane Cellars in Healdsburg, knows well. Her estate holiday homes welcome multi-generational groups regularly, and the thank-you notes she receives afterward tend to focus less on the wine than on the experiences.  

“They tell us about the special memories they created in the vineyards, in the pool, and at game time in the family room,” she says. When parents want time alone or a romantic dinner in town, grandparents take the children. When everyone gathers, the century-old vines are right there to explore. 

“This part of the country is so beautiful, and there are so many things to do,” DeGolia adds. “We love welcoming guests from all over the world to our little corner of the Russian River Valley.” (Healdsburg was recently named the Friendliest Town in America by WorldAtlas, a designation that feels fitting for a wine region increasingly oriented toward hospitality.)

After years of chasing younger drinkers through creative bottlings and branding, the wine industry may find that its most durable answer is not in the product but in the place. The most thoughtful estates understand that the child running through the vines today is not a distraction from the wine business. They are the wine business, twenty-something years from now. 

However, Zepponi says the goal is not to convert children into wine consumers, but to let them absorb wine as something woven into life and as a way that families can mark time together. “Those moments naturally leave a lasting impression,” he says. 

Even one afternoon of wine tasting with loved ones can evolve into a lifelong memory. My son lives thousands of miles away from me these days, but shared wine experiences make that distance feel a lot smaller. It’s proof that making room for kids on the vineyard can have lasting benefits, for families and winemakers.

title: Moscato d’Asti Is the Moment
summary:
Consorzio dell'Asti DOCG director Giacomo Pondini lets us in on why Moscato d'Asti is so much more than a "sweet wine."

The post Moscato d’Asti Is the Moment appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
This episode of the Wine Enthusiast Podcast is brought to you by the Consorzio dell’Asti DOCG.

Something is shifting in the way people drink wine. Nowadays, it feels less about chasing power or prestige and more about finding something that fits the moment. In that context, Moscato d’Asti is landing in a very interesting place.

For years, the wine was pigeonholed as simply sweet and fizzy. But that shorthand never told the full story. Today, as more people look for wines that are lighter, more flexible, and easier to enjoy, Moscato d’Asti starts to make a lot more sense. With alcohol levels often hovering around 5 percent, it delivers flavor, freshness, and lift without asking too much from the drinker.

In this conversation, Giacomo Pondini, director of Asti DOCG, pushes back on the idea that Moscato d’Asti needs to change. The better question is: Have we been looking at it the right way? The wine is about balance and precision, and that has only become more important as producers work through the realities of a warmer climate while trying to protect its signature vibrancy.

Moscato d’Asti is also a wine that deserves to be rethought at the table. Moving past the dessert-only labeling opens things up—pairing with spice, salt, heat, and even umami-driven dishes can bring out a completely different side of the wine. It becomes less about sweetness and more about energy and contrast.

There is also movement within the denomination itself. The arrival of Asti Rosé shows a willingness to explore new expressions while staying rooted in aromatic identity.

Moscato d’Asti has always had range. But right now, more people seem ready to see it.

On the basics of Moscato d’Asti

Giacomo Pondini: “Moscato d’Asti is not just a sweet wine—it’s a wine that is balanced, aromatic with a light sparkling, where the sweetness is not just the only component, it’s one of the components, because all these components in the end balance the outcome of the wine that you’re tasting. There’s a vibrant acidity with light effervescence that always enhances freshness. And the 5% usually that you find in Moscato d’Asti grape, 5% of alcohol, makes the wine so airy, not heavy. It’s always the right time to have a glass of Moscato d’Asti.”

On how Moscato d’Asti gets its signature effervescence

GP: “The fizziness comes from a specific way of producing this wine that actually was invented in the late 19th century by Federico Martinotti, a local oenologist that was helping wineries to produce a wine, a sparkling wine, that didn’t have to go through the two different moments of the classic method. Asti was born as a sparkling wine around 1865 in the classic method, but by the end of that century, there was the introduction of a new way of producing wine that was created by Federico Martinotti…. He helped in producing wine with the second fermentation directly in the tank. That was allowing producers to avoid all the losses of bottles that they were facing at the beginning of production, and that also allowed them to control the fermentation and stop the fermentation at a specific moment where they wanted a specific level of sugar, but most of all, when they wanted to have a lower alcohol percentage between five and six, which is the level of alcohol in Moscato d’Asti nowadays as well.”

On Moscato d’Asti’s Italian origins

GP: “It comes from the agricultural tradition. So that means that it became a wine seen by the Italian consumers, mainly, as a perfect match for desserts or sweet treats. But the thing is that, being born in the country, it was used by people who were living in the country, and they were pairing it—they probably didn’t even have enough money to buy the cake on Sunday, or maybe they could produce it at home, but they used that Moscato d’Asti to boost their day-to-day life, using it on their normal meals. It’s a traditional match/pairing here to use Moscato d’Asti with the so-called “merenda sinoira”— that moment, that brunch that you have when you come back home from working in the fields, and you need to boost yourself…. They used to eat bread, butter, and anchovies, and a glass of Moscato d’Asti that matches perfectly. The saltiness of the anchovies, the butteriness of the butter, the sweet tendency of the bread.”

On convincing consumers that it’s more than just a sweet wine

GP: “Well, it’s quite easy, because first of all, you just have to taste it, and then you realize that the wine perfectly matches every kind of pairing that you have in your mind. But at the same time, you can also have some more technical insight, saying that the sweetness of the wine is always balanced by a great acidity, a great freshness that doesn’t make the wine too heavy for your palate. And the sweetness that you may feel in that glass usually matches perfectly [with] food and dishes that are modern, that are spicy, that are hot. And you always search for something with more residual sugar when you have, for example, when you have a Chinese hotpot in front of you, or you have a Thai soup with that light spiciness that matches the sweetness of the Moscato. So we really like that. Moscato d’Asti is a contemporary wine that can match hundreds of food dishes from all over the world.”

On the appeal to low-alcohol-focused consumers

GP: “Before low-alcohol became a trend, Moscato d’Asti was already there. We’ve always been there, naturally, because this wine is not de-alcoholized. This wine has always been having the same level of alcohol because it comes from a tradition of winemaking that highlights the great characteristics of an aromatic grape called Moscato, and this wine reflects perfectly the taste of the berry of this grape. That means that you have an immediate connection to the berry, to the grapes, to the food that you eat. And so, in terms of representing the variety we will eat, Moscato d’Asti is the ideal choice to understand the character of a Moscato grape.”

On Moscato d’Asti’s ideal food pairing

Jeff Porter: “I know, for me, that one of the most interesting, kind of, savory pairings, and you alluded to it before, but like… Thai, Vietnamese, Southeast Asian cuisine. Because I think Moscato d’Asti, when I break it down in my head, as a sommelier and as a journalist—there’s the fruit component, which for me is normally peach, apricot, and nectarine. Then there’s an herbal component, which could be mint or then also maybe bay leaf, or something like sage, but definitely on that green spectrum. And then you’ve got just the vibrant, electric acidity that balances that sweetness.

But again, like when you get a really, really good spicy chili on your palate, and then you hit it, you get that herbaceousness, the fire from the chili. And this could be in Mexican cuisine, it could be in Thai. It could be in Calabrian cuisine. And then you get that splash of Moscato d’Asti, it just really sets everything right, where your palate just feels at one. I think that’s so special about food and wine pairing, and I think people have to look outside… beyond dessert with this wine.”

On recent developments in Asti DOCG

GP: “Well, the Asti DOCG recently made a change in the code of production, allowing also the producers of sparkling method to introduce a rosé, a style of sparkling Asti. Because we also introduced the use of Brachetto grapes, which are aromatic, which is an aromatic red variety, local red variety, that, just like Moscato, has a great history here in Piedmont. It’s always been together with Moscato—one of the traditional grapes always used in local productions—and we wanted to highlight this aromatic profile that makes these wines different from all the others. And we believe that also with that, with this new style of wine, we can be even more interesting for new generations, for consumers, for those who are looking for something low in alcohol, but really high in pleasure. So we believe that we are giving an answer to the market that is looking for something new, something… although there are many sparkling rosé wines, we believe that being aromatic, being made based on Brachetto and Moscato, this wine is going to have success on the market, on many markets all over the world.”

The Guest 

Giacomo Pondini is the director of the Consorzio dell’Asti DOCG. He has previously held the position of director at both the Consorzio del Vino Brunello di Montalcino and the Consorzio Tutela Morellino di Scansano.

More About the Podcast

The Wine Enthusiast Podcast is your serving of drinks culture and the people who drive it. You can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. Visit the podcast homepage for more episodes and transcripts.

Episode Transcript

Transcripts are generated using a combination of speech recognition software and human transcribers, and may contain errors. Please check the corresponding audio before quoting.

Speakers: Jeff Porter, Giacomo Pondini

Transcript:

Jeff Porter

Hello everybody. Welcome to the Wine Enthusiast podcast. My name is Jeff Porter. I’m a writer at large here at Wine Enthusiast, and today we are talking about Moscato d’Asti, the fun, vivacious fruit-forward wine from Piemonte, and we have Giacomo Pondini, the director of the Consorzio Asti DOCG, to tell us everything we don’t know.

Giacomo, it’s great to see you. It’s been a while—you and I have, in different lives, been around each other in the wine world for a long time, but I’m excited today to talk about Moscato d’Asti with you. It’s a historic wine, a wine that means a lot to me personally, as one of the first wines I had in my entire wine life. But welcome, welcome to the Wine Enthusiast podcast.

Giacomo Pondini 

Thank you. Thank you, Jeff, for having me. Thank you, Wine Enthusiast. I’m glad to keep working with you through our… I think it’s more – around 20 years that we’re knowing each other. So I’m super glad to keep talking with you.

JP 

You know, it’s interesting. I was reflecting back on, like, my experience with Moscato d’Asti, and I wanted to give you like, like, a nostalgia that really hit me. So when I was 21 years old, just freshly 21, because, you know, unlike here in Italy, 21 is what we have to be when we get to drink in the States legally, a friend of mine who lived in—for study abroad—in Italy, she came back and she’s like, “Dude, I got this whole new thing for us. Are you ready? Meet me at Zilker Park?” I lived in Austin, Texas at the time, and she showed up with prosciutto, which I’d never had before, a cantaloupe, which I’d had before, and a bottle of wine. And in Texas at that time, you couldn’t have alcohol at the park, so we were breaking… breaking the law. But she pulled the wine and I—it hit me like a garden and a peach orchard all at once, and it was a light bulb that went off from my head being like, “What is this?” One of the many things that steps… stepped me up into wine, you know. Is Moscato d’Asti more than just a sweet wine?

GP

Yeah, as you said, it is an explosion in your mouth and in your brain as well, because it’s… it’s a completely new, different style of wine that you expect from considering non-aromatic wines. Moscato d’Asti is not only a sweet wine, not just a sweet wine, it’s a wine that is balanced, aromatic with a light sparkling where the sweetness is not just the only component—it’s one of the components, because all these components in the end balance the outcome of the wine that you’re tasting. There’s a vibrant acidity with light effervescence that always enhances freshness. And the 5% usually that you find in Moscato d’Asti grape, 5% of alcohol, makes the wine so airy, not heavy. And it’s always the right time to have a glass of Moscato d’Asti.

JP

I think maybe let’s, let’s back up a little, and let’s situate ourselves. So Asti, I think: where are we? Who are you? And what is this, this wine that we’re talking about? How’s it made?

GP 

Well, the wine actually is named Asti because the appellation, the DOCG wine, which is the highest level of quality wines in Italy, is based on two styles of wine: the sparkling, which is Asti sparkling, and the fizzy, semi-sparkling wine, which is Moscato d’Asti. We’re talking about, mainly today, about Moscato d’Asti. And this wine is produced in an area in the northwest of Italy, in region Piedmont, in three different provinces. Asti, which is the central one, and then you also have the provinces of Alessandria and Cuneo, and 51 different municipalities are involved in the production of Moscato grapes, which are the only allowed for the production of Asti sparkling and Moscato d’Asti.

JP

And then so for the wine that we’re talking about today, Moscato d’Asti, like I know it as a fun, fizzy, fruity, exuberant, like… energetic wine. And it’s, it’s fizzy, as we say here in Italy, “frizzante,” right? And how does it get that fizziness?

GP

The fizziness comes from a specific way of producing this wine that actually was invented in the late 19th century by Federico Martinotti, a local oenologist that was helping wineries to produce a wine, a sparkling wine, that didn’t have to go through the two different moments of the classic method. Asti was born as a sparkling wine in around 1865 in the classic method, by the end of that century, there was the introduction of a new way of producing wine that was created by Federico Martinotti. He was working here as a consultant and oenologist, and he helped in producing wine with the second fermentation directly in the tank. That was allowing producers to avoid all the losses of bottles that they were facing at the beginning of production, and that also allowed to control the fermentation and stop the fermentation at a specific moment where they wanted a specific level of sugar, but most of all, when they wanted to have a lower alcohol percentage between five and six, which is the level of alcohol in Moscato d’Asti nowadays as well.

JP 

And about what year did we see the first Moscato d’Asti, as we know it, show up to the market?

GP

I think we have to go back to the 60s, around that time, where wineries were also trying to produce their own Moscato d’Asti with a specific way of filtering wines that were using old bags. We used to call them the Dutch bags. I don’t know if it sounds good in your language, but they used to filter wine, this sparkling wine, more and more as long as they could remove all the sugar residual in order to stop the fermentation. 

JP 

So we start to see it in the 60s. And, you know, when I first had it was interesting. I didn’t have it as, like, a dessert wine. I had it as a picnic wine, and it was sweet and it was fun and it was fruity, but, like, what was it originally used for? Because, like, it’s got a few hundred grams per liter of sugar in a bottle, correct?

GP

Exactly, but it comes from the agricultural tradition. So that means that it became a wine seen by the Italian consumers, mainly, as a perfect match for desserts or sweet treats. But the thing is that, being born in the country, it was used by people who were living in the country, and they were pairing it—they probably didn’t even have enough money to buy the cake on Sunday, or maybe they could produce it at home, but they use that Moscato d’Asti to boost their day to day life, using it on normal… on their normal meals. And it’s the typical tradition. It’s a traditional match, pairing here to use Moscato d’Asti with the so called “merenda sinoira”— that moment, that brunch that you have when you come back home from working in the fields, and you need to boost yourself and you… they used to eat bread, butter, and anchovies, and a glass for Moscato d’Asti that matches perfectly. The saltiness of the anchovies, the butteriness of the butter, the sweet tendency of the of the bread.

JP 

That’s amazing. So like, for those out there that don’t know, merenda is the snack that you get, like, after school or after work. So it’s the food in between dinner. And now, when I go have merenda, I’m 100% going to have anchovy, burro, and pane—the bread, butter, and anchovy and a glass of Moscato. I’ve never heard that and I love that.

So, you know, in the States, you know, everybody, for the most part, thinks of Moscato d’Asti as a sweet wine. And how do you convince a sweet wine skeptic that Moscato d’Asti can be for them? And more than just a sweet wine?

GP

Well, it’s quite easy, because first of all, you just have to taste it, and then you realize that the wine perfectly matches every kind of pairing that you have in your mind. But at the same time, you can also have some more technical insight, saying that the sweetness of the wine is always balanced by a great acidity, a great freshness that doesn’t make the wine too heavy for your palate. And the sweetness that you may feel in that glass usually matches perfectly food and dishes that are modern, that are spicy, that are hot, and you always search for something with more residual sugar when you have, for example, when you have a Chinese hotpot in front of you, or you have a Thai soup with that light spiciness that matches perfectly the sweetness of the Moscato. So we really like that. Moscato d’Asti is a contemporary wine that can match hundreds of food dishes from all over the world.

JP

And that’s… like when I try to explain it to people, it’s just like the essence of some of the most beautiful white peaches you could ever bite into. You know, when you get that perfect peach, and it’s got so much acidity that it doesn’t appear sweet at all, but you get the fruitiness of the peach, and then you get that hint of mint, a little herb, and then this rush of acidity. And I, you know, every time I have a glass of Moscato d’Asti, it’s just this… it’s like being in the best garden—in a glass. And I think that’s one of the most beautiful things about the wine.

In this moment of consumer introspection, in the growing market for like no and low alcohol wine, does Moscato d’Asti’s roughly 5% alcohol by volume set it up for success today?

GP

Absolutely. Before low alcohol became a trend, Moscato d’Asti was already there. We’ve always been there, naturally, because this wine is not de-alcoholized. This wine has always been having the same level of alcohol because it comes from a tradition of winemaking that highlights the great characteristics of an aromatic grape called Moscato, and this wine reflects perfectly the taste of the berry of this grape. That means that you have an immediate connection to the berry, to the grapes, from the food that you eat, and so, in terms of representing the variety we will eat, Moscato d’Asti is the ideal choice to understand the character of a Moscato grape.

JP

It is really interesting. I’m glad that you brought that up, because I think—I haven’t tried every grape on earth that makes wine, but I’ve tried a bunch of them in the vineyard and then in the glass, and I feel Moscato d’Asti, or the Moscato grape, is one of the few grapes that directly translates. When you eat that berry and then you have the wine a few months later, it’s almost the same. Whereas, like if you have Nebbiolo as a grape, and then you have Nebbiolo as a wine, even like Langhe Nebbiolo or something like that, there’s similar characteristics, but they’re pretty far apart, where Moscato d’Asti is like this 100% throughline.

GP

Yeah, sure. The second, third level of production, the vinification and the refining in the bottle usually tend to change the character of wine. With Moscato d’Asti, it’s completely different. The thing is that the wine, in the end, reflects, as you said, perfectly the grape that this wine is coming from, and this is also thanks to the technical production that has been allowed from the Martinotti method, because that really reflects perfectly, mirrors perfectly the character of the Moscato grape.

JP

With that and the production method, I’m going to talk about climate change. Moscato d’Asti is… one of the key characteristics is its acidity. That’s what keeps everything balanced. How do producers adapt to this ever-changing climate to ensure the product remains as energetic and vibrant and fresh as we experience it today?

GP

Yeah, it’s a tough challenge, because we all know that climate change is forcing our growers to, always, take more care of their vineyards. But sustainability is the key, of course. As a consortium, we also help vine growers to understand the right moment of picking, for example, the Moscato grapes. We have a large number of vineyards as a consortium. We check the maturation process, and we help to understand when is the right moment of picking. And we help vine growers to grow them, to grow grapes in a specific way in order to maintain the freshness, and to pick them at the right moment, because, always, acidity is the key. Usually, there’s also a third factor here in the Moscato growing area, and it’s the aromatic maturation, which always makes things harder for wine growers to decide the right moment of picking, because it doesn’t mean that the right moment of maturation in terms of sugar, is the exact moment also for in order to have the automatic the right aromatic profiles. So it’s always something that we are trying to support with our wine growers, and it’s something that really is important in order to showcase on the market—always the right level of quality for Moscato d’Asti.

JP

And that brings us, I think, to, again—Moscato d’Asti has one of the best quality to price ratios, in my opinion, for a bottle of wine. When you go to a wine shop or a restaurant in the United States, you can get the best versions of the wine at a price that is relatively affordable. With consumer wallets shrinking in our day and age, and more and more people, you know, spending the money on experiences and on various activities. Do you think there’s a pairing or a specific dish that really shows the identity of Moscato d’Asti?

GP

Well, I don’t know, we have mentioned a few pairings, but I always… one surprisingly, surprisingly fitting pairing that I find always interesting is pairing with some oysters, a glass of Moscato d’Asti with oysters. I know it’s not something that on a daily basis you may afford, but also having this good value wine doesn’t mean that you don’t have a luxury feel with pairing this wine with high end dishes. So it’s a… I think it deserves a try.

JP

And I think that’s, I mean, I can’t wait to try that. I mean, oysters and Moscato d’Asti, I haven’t done it. So next time I’m in Asti with you, you and me, let’s check some oysters and down some Moscato d’Asti. But in Piemonte—so, you gave us a traditional merenda dish. What’s another Piedmontese dish that you generally get with Moscato d’Asti?

GP

Well, for something more traditional, I would go… also within the tradition of pairing Moscato d’Asti with desserts, the Piedmont hazelnut cake. It’s always good to go because the hazelnut is another excellence coming out for Piedmont. So it’s always a winning match pairing this. This hazelnut cake, Piedmont hazelnut cake with Moscato d’Asti. And of course, we have hundreds of different cheeses around, so it’s always a perfect match, Moscato d’Asti and any kind of cheese that comes out from this beautiful region in terms of production.

JP

And you travel throughout the world to talk about Moscato d’Asti, and Moscato d’Asti is one of the best “made in Italy” exports globally. In your experience, what is your most surprising food pairing you’ve had with Moscato d’Asti that just really blew you away—that’s not Italian?

GP

Well, I was… last year, I was in New York at Zuma, having lunch with some journalists. And we all had a complete flight of Asian style food and… it’s really hard to recall one, one specific dish that was matching perfectly, but I remember the whole lunch was one of my favorite moments in my career of wine speech.

JP

And then, I know for me that one of the most interesting kind of, like savory pairings, and you alluded it to before, but like Thai, Vietnamese, Southeast Asian cuisine. Because I think Moscato d’Asti, when I break it down in my head, as a sommelier and as a journalist, there’s the fruit component, which for me is normally peach, apricot, nectarine. Then there’s an herbal component, which could be mint or then also maybe bay leaf, or something like sage, but definitely on that green spectrum.

And then you’ve got just the vibrant, electric acidity that balances that sweetness. But again, like when you get a really, really good spicy chili on your palate, and then you hit it, you get that herbaceousness, the fire from the chili. And this could be in Mexican cuisine, it could be in Thai. It could be in Calabrian cuisine. And then you get that, that splash of Moscato d’Asti, it just really sets everything right, where your palate just feels at one. I think that’s so special about food and wine pairing, and I think people have to look outside, beyond dessert with this wine.

GP

I totally agree. It’s really hard for me to think of just one simple, one single dish to match with Moscato d’Asti, but I believe you may find many of them.

JP

I mean, I think it’s a wine that just needs to be, maybe, experimented more with sommeliers and chefs globally and just see what they can do. Just like we did 20, 30 years ago with Riesling. You know, it wasn’t just—it started out as schnitzel and pfeffingen, and now, you know, you go to any great high sushi, Vietnamese restaurant, you’ll see Riesling on the wine list there.

GP

Totally agree, totally agree. We have a great chance in terms of interest from the market, in terms of what makes us different from other automatic wines.

JP

I wanted to circle back to affordability. How do you convince the market and the frugal consumers right now that this is the wine they need?

GP

In terms of positioning, you mean the price points?

JP

Yeah, this is an affordable wine, as we were talking about… is Moscato d’Asti the wine that frugal consumers need right now? So if you’re, if you want to get wine, and you want to make sure you save some money, but still have that experience at home, right? You know, is this the wine for those people?

GP

It’s the wine for those people because it matches many different moments of your daytime. You don’t have to see it just as a wine that can match your brunch or your aperitif, or any other moments. But just, especially in summertime, you just come out from work and you open up the bottle that you have in the fridge, and it’s so refreshing, so… refilling your spirit, that actually it’s good to go at any moment of your day. And at that price point, at that positioning on the market, you always have the feeling of drinking a good value product coming from a specific area—a natural product, non-processed wine, natural—in terms of sugar, in terms of carbon dioxide, nothing is added. And in terms of your, you know, not only your stomach will appreciate it, but also your feeling, your general feeling, will appreciate all these characteristics.

JP

And do you think that quality to price ratio also gives Moscato d’Asti—and the fact that it’s low on alcohol—an edge or an opportunity with younger wine consumers or non-wine consumers who we’re trying to bring into the community of drinking great wine?

GP 

Yeah, sure, in both ways, I believe that it can have success, both towards generations that are drinking wine in this moment, because in terms of alcohol, we’ve all been—as Italians, we’ve all been introduced to wine through Asti or Moscato d’Asti, as happened to you as well. And we believe that all the new generations can start appreciating wine from this unbelievable and unique style of wine.

JP

I know my daughter takes sneaky snips of it every so often, and so you’ve already got a good entry point in the Porter household with my daughter.

GP

It being her first wine, you never forget your first love. You know, it will always remain in her mind and our mind that Moscato d’Asti has a specific place in our wine tasting experience.

JP

And you know, earlier today, we were talking about some innovations and some, you know—it’s a historical wine region, but it’s not resting on its laurels. Is there any news that you’d like to give to the world out there about what’s going on in the Asti DOCG?

GP

Well, the Asti DOCG recently made a change in the code of production, allowing also the producers of sparkling method to introduce a rose, a style of sparkling Asti. Because we also introduced the use of Brachetto Grapes, which are aromatic, which is an aromatic red variety, local red variety, that just like Moscato, has a great history here in Piedmont. It’s always been together with Moscato, one of the traditional grapes always used in local productions, and we wanted to highlight this aromatic profile that makes these wines different from all the others. And we believe that also with that, with this new style of wine, we can be even more interesting for new generations, for consumers, for those who are looking for something low in alcohol, but really high in pleasure. So we believe that we are giving an answer to the market that is looking for something new, something—although there are many sparkling rose wines, we believe that being aromatic, being made based on Brachetto and Moscato, this wine is going to have success on the market, on many markets all over the world.

JP

Well, I’m excited for that, and I can’t thank you enough for your time today, Giacomo. I think talking about a historic, legendary wine that really speaks to modern times… it’s really special. I really appreciate your time today.

GP 

Thank you. I appreciated this as usual.

JP

I look forward to my next glass of Moscato d’Asti with you.

GP 

I look forward as well. Thank you, Jeff, thank you so much. Hope to meet you soon.

JP

This project is part of the promotional programme Asti DOCG experience — Campaign financed under Regulation EU n.2021/2115 and in accordance with Ministero dell’Agricoltura Italiano. With sweet or spicy, Moscato d’Asti is more than it seems. Have you tried any out of the box Moscato d’Asti pairings? Let us know. You can email us your comments and questions at podcast@WineEnthusiast.com. Remember, you can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. You can also go to WineEnthusiast.com/podcast for more episodes and transcripts.

This is Jeff Porter, thanks for listening.

The post Moscato d’Asti Is the Moment appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: Cool Canadians Spend Their Weekends In This Hidden Wine Region
summary:
Hidden between Toronto and Montreal, this hip wine region is flush with cool Canadian charm.

The post Cool Canadians Spend Their Weekends In This Hidden Wine Region appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Thirty years ago, there were no vines in Prince Edward County. Nestled between Toronto and Montreal, the island wine region on Lake Ontario was farm land, a hub for apple growing and tomato canning.

Then, drawn in by the Lake’s cool breezes and agricultural potential, winemakers arrived, buoyed by the belief that Chardonnay and Pinot Noir vines would love the region’s limestone– and clay-rich soils.

Over the years, others decamped to “The County” (as locals call it), attracted by its beaches, nascent winemaking scene, and budding reputation as a creative playground. 

This migration hit a fever pitch in 2020. Chefs, burnt out from the grind of urban dining scenes, opened restaurants in County towns like Consecon, Wellington, Bloomfield, and Picton. Architects followed, as did hoteliers, sommeliers, and designers. 

Now, Prince Edward County is buzzing—a pastoral paradise with a creative, cosmopolitan energy. There are paella restaurants and tasting rooms in restored Victorian houses, as well as new boutique hotels and Nordic spas. Of course, as this is an agricultural region, first and foremost, farm stands like Vicki’s Veggies are packed with wild riches and local cheeses.

I can personally confirm all of the above. My family calls The County home, so I’ve spent the last decade or so building out my perfect Prince Edward weekend. Here are the best places to drink, eat, and stay when trekking through this glorious section of Canadian wine country.

Where to Drink

Rosehall Run

Wellington

Rosehall Run’s owner and winemaker Dan Sullivan was one of the first to wave the County’s wine flag. 26 years later, Sullivan (alongside associate winemaker Lee Baker) has built a reputation for balancing classic expressions of Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir and hip trends like chilled reds and acid-rich Rieslings. The tasting room is bright and airy, with picnic tables overlooking the vineyard for en plein air sipping.

The Grange

Hillier

Don’t assume that The Grange’s 200-year-old barn is an indication of the estate’s winemaking mentality—this Hillier locale turns out some of the more ambitious wines in the County. Winemaker Jonas Newman’s Gamay, Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Cabernet Franc flex the potential of their terroir, and enjoying a glass by the barn’s roaring fireplace is the key to a perfect winter day.

Hinterland

Hillier

Newman’s personal project, Hinterland, is a dedicated sparkling house. Newman’s traditional method bubbles highlight how effectively Champagne styles can be reflected in Ontario. After all, Ontario is a cool climate region, and both Chardonnay and Pinot Noir thrive here. Though mostly Champenoise in style, Hinterland doesn’t take itself too seriously—summertime sees sparkling wine cocktails and slushies served on the barn’s back patio.

Stanners

Hillier

Father-son duo Cliff and Colin Stanners, both research scientists turned amateur winemakers (Cliff, by spending 20 years in a Montreal amateur winemaking club, Colin via a mini-winery he ran in his basement at UC Davis), now manage their own winery in Hillier.

After settling in the County, they planted Pinot Noir, Cabernet Franc, and Chardonnay. Now, they pour iterations of the above out of a rather groovy eco-friendly straw bale tasting room.

Trail Estates

Hillier

Mackenzie Brisbois’ organic and regenerative Trail Estate Winery embraces both New and Old World styles. Burgundian varieties are well represented (Brisbois makes serious Chardonnay) as are hybrid grapes which focus on the future of Canadian wines. Also up their sleeve: barrel-fermented Rieslings, expressive Cabernet Franc, serious sparkling wines, and Pét-Nats for the cool-kid crowd.

Russ & Co.

Picton

Despite its rural area code, Russ & Co. has big city energy. Cocktails play with inquisitive flavors and use serious techniques, like tequila fizzes made with carrot, egg white, and habanero, and vegetal, fresh Vespers made with dill and celery. The interiors follow suit with their charming eclecticism, full of bric-a-brac decor and bistro chairs.

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

Where to Eat

Theia

Picton

Partners-in-life-and-business Myles Harrison and Margot Kenny run Theia, a deeply charming 25-seat wine bar just off Picton’s main thoroughfare. Plates are small and snackable, done thoughtfully with a focus on local farms. The wine program follows a similar ethos—think deep cuts from around the globe and local producers worth paying attention to.

Bloomfield Public House

Bloomfield

Every visit to Bloomfield Public House is full of surprises—the menu rotates at a rapid clip, dictated by what’s fresh, fun, and seasonal. Some days, that means potato croquettes topped with beef tartare. Other days, it’s char-grilled quail on a bed of root veggies. Even with the modifications, there’s consistency: the service is always warm and welcoming. Like Theia’s, Bloomfield’s wine list rallies for local producers.

Slake Brewing

Picton

Looking for a beer-forward change of pace? Slake Brewing is the place to be. The farm brews out lagers and ales made with craft, dedication, and all-Ontario malt, plus small-batch sours often aged in their house cellar. The beers taste even better from their tasting room, perched on a limestone hill overlooking the entire county. Rebel Taco prepares Tex-Mex fare onsite to soak it all up. My advice: roll up for golden hour, grab a beer and a taco, and take in one of the County’s best sunsets.

Bocado

Picton

After cutting his teeth in Toronto’s Mediterranean restaurant scene, Australian chef Stuart Cameron chose County life in 2021, opening Spanish-inspired Bocado in Picton. Though his dishes wear their Iberian influences on their sleeves, they’re imbued with big-time Canadian countryside energy. Start with local cheeses and charcuterie, move into croquetas and Galician-style octopus, then finish with a table-filling paella.

Where to Stay

The Royal Hotel

Picton

Seven years ago, The Royal Hotel was in ruins. Birds were its main tenants, and a grand spiral staircase that was once the focal point of the former railway hotel (built in 1879) was covered in moss and all but condemned. First-time hoteliers Sol Korngold and Greg Sorbara bet on the space’s potential and laboriously restored it, even importing bricks from Europe to match the hotel’s original form.

Now, The Royal is bright and airy, washed in warm wood and finished with clever architectural details that nod to its Victorian heritage. Guests hang by the pool and its adjacent trailer, which serves cocktails and County bubbles. Locals linger over wine in the lobby or over pizza in the front bar, while plates of pasta and hyper-seasonal dishes are served in the more formal restaurant space. Chef Albert Ponzo sources ingredients from both his family farm and Korngold’s farm, and cherry-picks produce from the County’s bounty.

The Manse

Picton

First built in 1906, this magnificent building has been carefully tweaked and updated. Now, it’s a charming nine-bedroom B&B known as The Manse Boutique Inn. Historical details like exposed limestone walls and soaring turret ceilings are balanced by creature comforts like jet bathtubs, pools, and hot tubs. Chef Max Dallmore’s dinners are artful and cheeky—expect dishes like fancied-up poutine made with confit potatoes, smoked salmon, caviar, and mushroom garum.

Claramount Club

Picton

Over the last few years, the Claramount Club’s Colonial waterfront estate (built in 1904) has been refreshed into a swish lakeside hotel. The original home was transformed into a set of suites, with maximalist wallpaper, colorful furnishings, and design details that highlight the history of the property and region (like original photographs of locals leisuring by the lake from the early ‘90s). Waterfront suites are more modern in design, and overlook the bay. A tennis court, heated pool, lakeside fire pits, and a tricked-out gym add to the appeal, as do the estate’s two ambitious restaurants.

Fronterra Farm

Consecon

This regenerative flower farm at the entrance to North Beach Provincial Park has a selection of off-grid glamping experiences overlooking an old forest. But a stay at Fronterra Farm certainly isn’t roughing it—every tent includes a king-sized bed, a covered outdoor kitchen, and an outdoor lounge with a fireplace. During the summer season, the farm’s cut-your-own-flower experience allows you to bring the destination home with you.

  • These dreamy guest houses will transport you to vineyard living.
  • Planning a trip to Napa? Make sure these wineries are on your list.
  • And here are the best wineries in Long Island to visit right now.
  • In case you hadn’t heard: There’s so much more to Bordeaux than Bordeaux. 
  • The cocktail scene in Warsaw is electric—and totally worth planning a trip around.
  • And then there’s Vancouver, which is full of world-class bars and excellent dining.

title: Take Classic Spanish Dishes to the Next Level with These Wine Pairings
summary:
Wine experts weigh in on the best wine pairings for beloved Spanish dishes, from refreshing gazpacho to garlicky gambas.

The post Take Classic Spanish Dishes to the Next Level with These Wine Pairings appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
In Spain, pairing wine with food is as much an art form as the curves and swirls of Antoni Gaudí’s Modernist architecture or Pablo Picasso’s Cubist masterpieces. Whether it’s skewers of pintxos at the bar washed down with crisp, chilled glasses of Txakolina, plates of tapas enjoyed with a communal porrón of vermouth passed around a cafe table, or an elevated tasting menu with vertical flights of Gran Reserva Rioja, Spaniards wholeheartedly embrace the idea that wine is woven into the rhythm and the ritual of the meal itself.

Luckily, you don’t have to jet off to San Sebastián or Barcelona to experience Iberian culinary synergy. We asked Spanish wine professionals to share their strategies, favorite varieties, and go-to styles for six of the country’s most beloved dishes.

The Basics of Pairing Spanish Food and Wine

“Spanish cuisine is deeply regional, and the best pairings often come from the same origin,” says Victor Del Castillo, assistant general manager at Lucia Marbella, a Mediterranean beachfront restaurant in the Don Carlos Hotel in Marbella, Spain. “Fresh, coastal dishes tend to pair well with crisp whites and fortified wines, while richer, inland dishes are better suited to structured reds.” So, while freshly-shucked oysters in Galicia scream for a high-acid, saline-tinged Albariño, that same wine would be lost alongside roast suckling lamb in Ribera del Duero, which needs the robust, dark fruit notes of a grape like Tempranillo.

Ryan Hazzard, wine director at Casa Teresa, a rustic, wood-fired Spanish restaurant in Washington, D.C., agrees that while tradition and regionalism are important factors in pairing wines with Spanish dishes, the availability these days to such a wide array of wines easily lends itself to fork and cork experimentation. “There’s a balance between the old and the new that can be explored by simply tasting the classics and learning a little about their history.”

However, the entire small plates concept is predicated on the notion of tasting a lot of different flavors, ingredients, techniques, and preparations during the course of one meal. So are there sure-fire pairings that work with all Spanish dishes? Yes, says Jordi Paronella, wine director at José Andrés Group. “Try sparkling wines like Cava and Corpinnat from the Penedès region which have aromas of green apples, Mediterranean herbs, and an almond character,” he says. He’s also partial to fino and manzanilla sherry, which he praises for their dry, briny, salty, and nutty notes that go with just about anything.

Paella with Mencía

Valencia’s iconic dish is prepared over an open-flame in a wide, shallow pan called a paellera, using short-grain rice that’s scented (and colored) with saffron threads. While the original recipe includes rabbit and chicken, there’s also a variation with seafood. But no matter the ingredients, the most coveted bite is the socarrat, the crispy, caramelized layer of rice on the bottom of the pan. With the traditional version, Katie Button, chef and co-owner of Cúrate Bar de Tapas in Asheville, North Carolina, opens a bottle from Bierzo in the northwest of the province of León. “Mencía has ripe red fruit, earthy undertones, floral notes, and minerality,” she says. “Its herbal quality mirrors the rosemary in the paella, and the lighter red pairs perfectly with rabbit.”

Pan Con Tomate with Albariño or Godello

This Catalan staple ingeniously uses toasted bread like a cheese grater, onto which is rubbed a clove of garlic and a cut tomato. Finished with a drizzle of good oil and a sprinkle of sea salt (and sometimes anchovies or a paper thin slice or two of jamón), it’s the best open-faced sandwich you’ll ever eat. For Spanish dishes with tomatoes, Hazzard says “a more austere Albariño or Godello further amplifies the dish’s acidic lift without diminishing the savory-sweet flavor of fresh tomato.” Paronella agrees with the appeal of Albariño as a versatile pairing for tapas. “With nectarine, peach, chamomile and some sea breeze, it’s clean, crisp, and refreshing.”

Tortilla Española with Oloroso Sherry

Deceptively simple in its ingredients, this dish does require a bit of technique. Diced potatoes (and often onions) are fried confit-style in a generous amount of olive oil and cooked with beaten eggs until just-set, before the whole mixture is carefully flipped. It’s ubiquitous in Spain, where it’s sliced into wedges or squares and served warm or at room temperature. Button always reaches for an oloroso sherry. “The caramel notes in the wine mimic the caramelized onions in the tortilla, while the salinity cuts through the egg and the nuttiness pairs with the browned potato,” she says. “It might be my favorite Spanish wine and food pairing, full stop.”

Croquetas with Cava Brut Nature

These fried pillows of deliciousness actually originated in France as a way to use up leftovers. Eventually they were brought over to Spain, where they were perfected and became a staple at tapas bars from Madrid to Seville. The crunchy snacks are usually filled with creamy béchamel sauce and jamón and seasoned with nutmeg or onion, but you’ll also find ones stuffed with cheese, chicken, or mushrooms. The effervescence in a bone dry Cava brut nature scrub the palate after each bite, while whetting the palate for the next one. “The precision and elegance of fine bubbles cut through the creaminess, creating a refined and balanced experience,” says Del Castillo. If you don’t want something fully sparkling, try slightly fizzy, zesty Txakoli.

Gambas al Ajillo with Rosado

Succulent shrimp quickly sautéed in good olive oil with copious amounts of fresh garlic and chili flakes for a little heat: what’s not to love? It’s one of the first dishes that comes to mind when anyone mentions tapas—with good reason. While you might assume that a Spanish white would cozy up best, Hazzard encourages you to think pink, in the form of a fruit-forward rosado made from Garnacha or Tempranillo. “Softer, lighter, fruitier wines work best when garlic is a primary component in a dish,” he says, since its sulfur compounds can amplify the tannins in reds. “And I prefer to add a little more fruit to the pairing than most white wines can offer.”

Gazpacho with Verdejo

During hot Andalusian summers, this chilled soup is liquid air conditioning. Whether served blended and smooth or rustic and chunky, in a glass or a bowl, it’s chock full of so many of the flavors of Spain: tomatoes, olive oil, garlic, vinegar, and fresh vegetables. It needs a wine that parallels its zippy, tangy profile, according to Del Castillo. “Verdejo’s herbal and citrus notes complement the acidity of the dish,” he says. “Its aromatic profile mirrors its freshness and enhances its vegetal and citrus nuances.” If you’re really jonesing for a red, go with a lightly chilled, fruity, low-tannin Garnacha.

title: Can Organic and Biodynamic Farming Actually Improve Yields?
summary:
Recent research sheds new light on farming practices once viewed as overly laborious and expensive.

The post Can Organic and Biodynamic Farming Actually Improve Yields? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Doing the right thing in the vineyard is often seen as a compromise. Chemical-free farming means a lower carbon footprint and cleaner water and soils, but it also requires more hands-on work in the vineyard, and, conventional wisdom goes, lower yields. In other words, organic and biodynamic farming is economically costly. 

But is that really true? 

As our climate becomes increasingly unpredictable with more frequent and longer-lasting extreme weather events, as well as day-to-day temperature changes exceeding the 90th percentile threshold of historical records, new studies and anecdotal evidence from data-gathering viticulturalists are challenging longstanding perceptions surrounding organic and biodynamic farming.

An 18-year-field trial conducted in Germany assessed yields, vine vigor, and grape quality in conventional, organic, and biodynamic vineyards. At first, conventional won out, with organic and biodynamic farming systems showing yield deficits of -17% and -14% respectively. But those gaps diminished considerably over time, and about a decade after conversion, organic and biodynamic yields improved by 2.3% and 9% respectively during hot and dry vintages. Ravaz values, which measure vine balance and grape quality, were also higher in organic and biodynamically farmed systems. 

Another study through the University of Alicante found that organic farming boosted crop yields, soil health, and biodiversity across 179 crops in eight countries covering a range of climates and soils.

Jason Haas, the second-generation proprietor at the organic-since-launch Tablas Creek, can’t speak to comparisons between farming systems, but he has observed differences during tough vintages—2022, most notably—between his vineyards and that of his conventionally farmed neighbors in Paso Robles, California. 

“We felt like 2022 was a great example of how our regenerative organic farming helped us,” Haas says. “The water-holding capacity in our soils allowed the vines to make it through the first mid-summer heat spike without many of the issues we heard about in our neighbors, such as raisining and sunburn.”

While organic and biodynamic farming are often discussed together and share similarities, they also have key differences. Organic farming is a regulated system that is focused on eliminating chemical and synthetic inputs. In the United States, the USDA Organic program offers certification. 

Biodynamic farming also eliminates chemical and synthetic inputs, but places more of an emphasis on biodiversity by treating the farm as a self-sustaining living ecosystem with a variety of plants and animals integrated into the farm. Demeter International is the main certification program for biodynamics across the world.

“Chemicals beget chemicals,” says winemaker and general manager Vance Rose, of Sonoma’s Harper’s Rest. “One of my favorite things about organic farming as a lazy winemaker is that I don’t have to do anything in the cellar. I don’t want to add chemicals or use commercial yeast, and when I get organic grapes that have flourished even in tough vintages, I don’t have to.”

Surviving the ‘Armageddon Vintage’

Even the most altruistic farmer hopes for a great harvest, and compromising on yield in favor of environmental principals feels like a steep price to pay.

But Rose has seen only upside. He says that his time in the vineyards has proven beyond a doubt how important organic farming is, especially in 2022. 

“We measured the soil in our vineyard and compared it to the soil in our conventionally farmed neighbors, and ours was 33% cooler, thanks to the higher water-holding capacity, cover crops, and overall health,” Rose says. “When the temperature of the soil hits a certain temperature, the vines shut down, and the grapes stop receiving nutrients.”

At Palisades Canyon Wines in the Napa Valley, winemaker and grape-grower Graeme MacDonald refers to 2022 as a “horrific year across the board. Most people in Napa had fried fruit. It felt like the Armageddon vintage. But because of our organic farming practices, and our dry-farmed head-trained vineyards, we just didn’t get the raisined grapes and volatile acidity we saw across the Valley.”

You’re only as good as your worst year, MacDonald notes. And in 2022, he was proud to present what he considered a great wine made possible by organic farming. 

At Napa’s Bella Oaks, winemaker Nigel Kinsman agrees that 2022 was a revelation.

“The resilience during extreme vintages is especially clear,” Kinsman says. “Our organically farmed vineyards weathered the heat beautifully. The grapes in neighboring vineyards were withered, and their yields were way down. The differences between the resulting wines were especially stark. If you get shitty fruit, there’s only so much triage you can do.”

Better Terroir, Better Wines

Advocates of organic and biodynamic wines argue that it’s much easier to produce wines that reflect the terroir, without any tampering, than it is with conventionally farmed wines. (They may have a point: studies show that organic wines are scored higher by critics). 

For Reid Griggs, vintner at Burgess Cellars, the quality in the glass comes down to the soil the grapes are grown in. Chemicals incinerate microbial communities in the soil, and Griggs says these microscopic creatures are essential for great wine.

“Every soil’s microbial fingerprint is unique,” Griggs says. “These communities are capable of transforming the fruit, and they help encourage a thriving ecosystem, starting with the soil.”

Wine is a highly complex chemical mixture with over one thousand compounds. While we don’t yet have the tools to assess the precise impact of organic farming on how these compounds interact, to Griggs, it’s clear. 

“We can’t chemically measure balance, or the taste of a wine that is alive, but wines grown in thriving ecosystems are more distinct and impart more complex flavors that are specific to the site,” he says. 

The promise of making wine that emerges not just unscathed, but beautifully, from a difficult vintage is likely enough for more winemakers to get on the organic bandwagon, especially when yield isn’t diminished.

An additional consideration is long-term longevity. “Conventional farming has reduced the lifespan of a U.S. vineyard to around 30 years,” says Hamel Family Wines director of winegrowing John Hamel, who converted his family’s Sonoma vineyards to certified organic and biodynamic in 2015. “Yet across Sonoma and the North Coast, we see heritage vineyards planted in the late 1800s remain healthy and productive today.”

These ancient heritage vineyards were established before modern irrigation and chemical farming were even possible, Hamel notes. 

They stand as a living testament to the long-term economic power of organic, holistic farming. And with climate change menacing seemingly every harvest, this style of farming also brings palpable short-term benefits that serve growers, vintners, and wine drinks alike.

title: What Wine Reviewers Are Drinking This Summer
summary:
From Lebanese whites to chillable Beaujolais, here are the bottles our reviewers are reaching for this summer.

The post What Wine Reviewers Are Drinking This Summer appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Summer drinking doesn’t only have to mean crisp whites and bubbly rosé. In fact, the Wine Enthusiast Tasting Department and its cadre of Writers-at-Large are adamant that the best wines are for year-round enjoyment. 

Of course, this wine-obsessed crew also delights in bottlings that pair beautifully with warm weather. We asked our reviewers for deeper insights into what they’re drinking this summer, and their answers were all over the place, quite literally: from rich white Rioja to fresh Albariño from Uruguay to dry, aromatic Finger Lakes Riesling. 

If you’re looking to stock your own wine fridge this summer, take cues from our pros and check out these bottles.

White Rioja

“Here in Lisbon, I’m always sipping great Portuguese pours, but as the weather warms this year, I’m kinda feeling it’s gonna be my summer of white Rioja. The diversity of styles, varieties, and terroir going on with the whites has become just phenomenal. You can luxuriate over a rich, oak-driven bottling at dinner or crush stuff that’s bright and minerally on a sunny weekend picnic.” – Writer-at-Large John Sumners, who reviews wines from Portugal and Northeast Spain

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Bodega Bideona 2023 Cabezadas Viura (Rioja)

Fresh, mineral and mouthwatering, led by aromas of flint and grapefruit pith, carrying into flavors crisp apple, firm nectarine and a squeeze of salted lemon. Delicious and versatile at the table. Best Buy. 92 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

 

$20 Saratoga Wine

Shop Now

Picpoul de Pinet

“On the Old World front, I’m pretty fired up on the Picpoul de Pinets I am reviewing from the Languedoc region of southern France: full of acid yet with ample stonefruit flavor, basically all you need for shellfish, the beach, or the poolside.” — Writer-at-Large Matt Kettman, who reviews wines from California (Central Coast & Southern California) and Languedoc-Roussillon

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Les Filets 2024 Piquepoul Blanc (Picpoul de Pinet)

Apricot and nectarine aromas pick up hints of beeswax and yellow rose on the rather complex nose of this bottling. The palate has a bit more heft than others in this category, presenting stonefruit, citrus and coconut flavors. Best Buy. 92 Points — M.K.

South America’s Less-Famous Whites

“This summer, I’m going beyond Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc to explore South America‘s diverse white wines, including Sémillon, Torrontes, white blends, and Albariño. I’m starting with the fresh yet layered Bodega Garzón 2024 Petit Clos Albariño from Uruguay’s coastal Maldonado region.”  – Writer-at-Large Jesica Vargas, who reviews wines from Latin American and Africa.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Bodega Garzón 2024 Petit Clos Albariño (Maldonado)

Uruguayan Albariño continues to impress year after year. From Maldonado, near the Atlantic coast, this white wine shows complexity, depth, and refreshing acidity, balancing a combination of delicious flavors. Flowers, honey, and sweet pear on the nose give way to white fruit, ginger, and subtle herbal notes on the lively palate. Editor’s Choice. 94 Points — J.V.

Inquire with winery

Dry Riesling from the Finger Lakes

“After the coldest, snowiest winter in recent memory here in New York, summer could not come sooner. While I crack open white wine any time of the year, my consumption of it probably triples in the hotter months. Dry Riesling plays an especially prominent role on my back deck table thanks to its vivid, thirst-quenching acidity and food friendliness. I have my eye on one from a new(ish) producer on Seneca Lake in the Finger Lakes (my birthplace!) called Ria’s. Their 2024 ‘Viva’ is an aromatic, lemongrass-and-lime blossom strewn bottling with the variety’s hallmark line of racy acidity and lovely slippery texture that ticks all my summer-sipping boxes.” — Writer-at-Large Christina Pickard, who reviews wines from Australia, New Zealand, England, and New York

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Ria’s Wines 2024 Viva Riesling (Seneca Lake)

From an exciting new husband-and-wife team, this lakeside Riesling offers delicate aromas of lemongrass, green tea and lime blossom that become more bombastic with time aerating in glass. In the mouth, it’s a lovely combo of racy lemon pith acidity and slippery textural weight. Still young, this could evolve quite beautifully and complexly over time. Editor’s Choice. 95 Points — C.P.

$30 WineDeals.com

Chillable Reds

“While white wine gets lots of attention during the summer, I really crave low-alcohol, chillable reds during the warmer months. Beaujolais checks all the boxes with bright, fresh acidity while not sacrificing structure. Just give it a quick chill in the fridge for 30 minutes, or put it in a bucket of ice like you would a white wine or rosé and give it time to warm up a bit in your glass.” —Writer-at-Large Reggie Solomon, who reviews wines from France, Spain, and other regions

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Château des Jacques 2022 Morgon

This wine engages and enlivens the senses. Rhubarb and cherry-cola aromas begin on the nose followed by hints of dried-cranberry skin and forest floor. Tannins are refined and soulful on the palate. Pair with chicken paillard, skirt steak and mushroom risotto. 92 Points — R.S.

White Blends from Lebanon

Few wines bring the oh-hey-now-it’s-an-occasion vibes quite like this 2018 white blend from Lebanon’s iconic Château Musar. Serve it with an at-home mezze spread featuring peak summer produce or a Costco chicken; either way, you won’t be disappointed.” — Writer-at-Large Emily Saladino, who reviews wines from Croatia, Georgia, Greece, Hungary, and Lebanon.

Château Musar 2018 White Blend (Bekaa Valley)

The beautifully textured palate of this creamy, amber-hued wine has nuanced lemon, tangerine, clove, honey, and gently oxidative flavors. Expect velvety tannins on the long, citrus-driven finish. 96 Points — E.S.

title: 30 Summer Cocktails to Help Beat the Heat
summary:
From Mai Tais and Micheladas to mocktails and margaritas, these refreshing summer sippers will keep you cool all season long.

The post 30 Summer Cocktails to Help Beat the Heat appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
If there’s one thing we can all agree on, it is that summer is prime time for enjoying mixed drinks al fresco. When it’s 75 degrees outside and the sun stays high in the sky late into the evening, indulging in a bright summer cocktail (or mocktail) just feels right. So what is the best warm season sipper? With so many good options, the answer to that question is much less obvious. 

Of course, there are the classics. The Aperol spritz is light and refreshing with just enough syrupy bitterness. The Piña Colada—refreshingly sweet and boozy both frozen and on the rocks—is practically synonymous with balmy weather. 

If you want to mix it up beyond the tried and true, options abound. Shake up a spicy mezcalita—the Tajín rim is not optional—for the perfect blend of salty, smoky, spicy, and citrusy flavors. Or, combine red wine and cola to create the aperitif-appropriate Spanish Kalimotxo. 

Going alcohol free this summer? We’ve got that covered too with non-alcoholic spirit replacements and mocktails that are non-alcoholic by design. 

No matter what you choose, these 30 downright delicious cocktails are certain to cool you down and quench your thirst all season long.

Tequila and Mezcal: The Unofficial Summer Spirits

Classic Margarita

It’s generally accepted that the iconic margarita evolved from the Daisy cocktail. The jury is still out on the original creator, this margarita combining blanco tequila, orange liquor, and lime juice is as classic and complete as they come.

Mezcalita

OK, fine—Tajín is technically optional, but we highly recommend it here (and always). When combined with smoky mezcal and tart lime juice, the salty-spicy rim takes the mezcalita to the next level. 

Ranch Water

This Texas summertime staple uses three ingredients—tequila, lime, and sparkling water—for a refreshing, easy cocktail that basically goes down like water.

Mezcal Negroni

Smoky and bitter, the mezcal Negroni works any time of year. But the addition of agave somehow makes it better suited for warm weather than the OG. In our opinion, it undeniably tastes best when sipped on the sidewalk in denim cutoffs and flip flops, as the sun sets and a feel-good soundtrack plays in the background.

Paloma

This bubbly, grapefruit-forward Paloma is all about simplicity. Combining tequila, grapefruit juice, and soda water, it works just as well at brunch time as it does on the beach with your toes in the sand.

Tequila Sunrise

Orange juice, grenadine, and blanco tequila are the stars in the sweet Tequila Sunrise.

The Guavabonga

Packed with pineapple, guava, and orange, this zesty mezcal cocktail is a refreshing foil to the summer heat. 

In the Shop

Upcycled Wine Bottle Highball Glasses (Set of 6) – Clear

In Stock | $59.99

Shop Now

Spritzes, Wine Cocktails, and Everything in Between

Aperol Spritz

We could wax poetic about the Aperol spritz’s many merits forever. Perfectly balanced and supremely refreshing, from brunch to beach to bar, this drink suits any and every warm weather occasion.

Hugo Spritz

Similar to the Aperol spritz but with sweet elderflower flair, Hugo spritz is a refreshing, low-alcohol sparkling wine cocktail with a gentle, floral flavor and a hint of herbaceous mint.

Sangria

Sangria variations are truly endless, but you can’t go wrong with a classic Tempranillo sangria. This batched recipe features a full bottle with pomegranate juice, lemon-lime soda, brandy, and the traditional fruit.

Limoncello Spritz

Looking for a new refreshing, low-abv spritz? We’ve got you covered. The limoncello spritz is simple, fruity, and delicious.

White Wine Spritzer

This fizzy wine and soda cocktail has been an iconic summertime symbol since the ’70s. Throw in a few jalapeño slices with Sauvignon Blanc or some frozen peaches for a modern twist.

Bellini

Forget those canned juice cocktails from bottomless brunch. The traditional Bellini recipe leans on fresh peaches for a sensational sparkling mix.

Kalimotxo

The Kalimotxo, also known as the Calimocho, is beloved across Spain. Made with a 3:1 ratio of red wine and cola, it’s about time the rest of the world embraces this simple and delicious summer slammer.

Michelada

The michelada is a choose-your-own-adventure beer cocktail. While the Mexican lager base is non-negotiable, you can tweak the recipe from there to make it as salty, citrusy, or spicy as you please.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Vienna Break-Resistant Cabernet Sauvignon Wine Glass-Set of 4

In Stock | $100

Shop Now

All Things Tropical and Tiki

Piña Colada

Popularized in the 1960s and ‘70s, the Piña Colada blends tart pineapple and creamy coconut for a tropical-style cocktail perfect for any day in the sun.

Rum Punch

A single serving of this spicy and fruity rum punch might seem contradictory. But fear not! This recipe easily scales up to punch bowl size, and doing so will make you the most popular person at any party.

Modern Strawberry Daiquiri

The classic poolside strawberry daiquiri gets a glow-up in this modern twist. The spicy-smokey flavors of mezcal and Fernet elegantly elevate with the classic strawberry and lime base.

Mai Tai

A tiki mainstay, the Mai Tai combines two types of rum with citrus-forward ingredients and nutty orgeat syrup. No matter where you are, one sip of this tropical classic and you’ll be conjuring images of palm trees and white-sand beaches in seconds.

USS Bomber

Hailing from New York City’s Maison Premiere, this unique cocktail’s combination of coconut milk and blue curacao is totally tiki, while its ombre layers are perfectly patriotic for holiday weekend parties.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Brilliance! Shatterproof Tritan Ribbed Recycled Outdoor Wine Glasses Set of 6 Assorted Color

In Stock | $59.95

Shop Now

Refreshing and Fruit-Forward

Pimm’s Cup

The classic Pimm’s Cup is a 1:2 ratio of Pimm’s No. 1 and sparkling English lemonade. The cucumber, apple, strawberry, and mint garnishes are essential. 

Salty Dog

A mix of grapefruit juice and gin or vodka, the Salty Dog is served over ice with a salted rim. This puckery, saline, cooling beverage might just be the perfect highball variation to beat the summer heat.

Blackberry Bourbon Smash

Few ingredients can brighten up bourbon like blackberries and mint. This fresh and fruity cocktail proves, yet again, that whiskey can go down well year-round.

Mojito

Tainted by years of overly-muddled sugar bombs and sordid reputations among bartenders, the classic lime, mint, and rum mojito is shockingly simple and refreshing when made properly. 

Watermelon Mojito

This watermelon mojito features fresh fruit juice and vibrant ginger beer for mojito lovers who want a little more refreshing complexity. Need we say more?

Gin Daisy

A predecessor to the margarita, this gin-spiked sipper is reminiscent of the now-ubiquitous agave cocktail—minus the tequila.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Brilliance! Shatterproof Tritan Ribbed Recycled Stemless Wine Glasses (Set of 6) – Assorted Colors

In Stock | $49.95

Shop Now

No Alcohol Doesn’t Mean No Fun

Non-Alcoholic Smoky Paloma

With a smoke-treated glass and homemade sage simple syrup, this non-alcoholic smoky Paloma riff is guaranteed to impress. 

The Sober Spritz

The Sober Spritz combines a non-alcoholic aperitif with elderflower syrup and sparkling water for a summery NA riff on the herbal and floral Hugo Spritz. 

The Detox

An intriguing combination of pineapple and celery, the Detox comes together with a savory dash of sea salt.

Non-Alcoholic Margarita

Fresh citrus and non-alcoholic tequila keep this virgin margarita recipe balanced.

In the Shop

Jupiter Gold Rimmed Iced Beverage Glasses (Set of 6)

In Stock | $72

Shop Now

  • Stir some sherry into the classic Paloma for an effortlessly delicious, low-proof cocktail.
  • This just in: Protein has come for cocktails.
  • Want to drink like a movie star? Shake up the official Oscars afterparty cocktails.
  • We’re begging you: Stop asking ChatGPT for drink recommendations.
