title: What Wine Reviewers Are Drinking This Summer
summary:
From Lebanese whites to chillable Beaujolais, here are the bottles our reviewers are reaching for this summer.

The post What Wine Reviewers Are Drinking This Summer appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Summer drinking doesn’t only have to mean crisp whites and bubbly rosé. In fact, the Wine Enthusiast Tasting Department and its cadre of Writers-at-Large are adamant that the best wines are for year-round enjoyment. 

Of course, this wine-obsessed crew also delights in bottlings that pair beautifully with warm weather. We asked our reviewers for deeper insights into what they’re drinking this summer, and their answers were all over the place, quite literally: from rich white Rioja to fresh Albariño from Uruguay to dry, aromatic Finger Lakes Riesling. 

If you’re looking to stock your own wine fridge this summer, take cues from our pros and check out these bottles.

White Rioja

“Here in Lisbon, I’m always sipping great Portuguese pours, but as the weather warms this year, I’m kinda feeling it’s gonna be my summer of white Rioja. The diversity of styles, varieties, and terroir going on with the whites has become just phenomenal. You can luxuriate over a rich, oak-driven bottling at dinner or crush stuff that’s bright and minerally on a sunny weekend picnic.” – Writer-at-Large John Sumners, who reviews wines from Portugal and Northeast Spain

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Bodega Bideona 2023 Cabezadas Viura (Rioja)

Fresh, mineral and mouthwatering, led by aromas of flint and grapefruit pith, carrying into flavors crisp apple, firm nectarine and a squeeze of salted lemon. Delicious and versatile at the table. Best Buy. 92 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

 

$20 Saratoga Wine

In the Shop

6-Bottle Waxed Canvas Weekend Wine Bag

In Stock | $79.95

Shop Now

Picpoul de Pinet

“On the Old World front, I’m pretty fired up on the Picpoul de Pinets I am reviewing from the Languedoc region of southern France: full of acid yet with ample stonefruit flavor, basically all you need for shellfish, the beach, or the poolside.” — Writer-at-Large Matt Kettman, who reviews wines from California (Central Coast & Southern California) and Languedoc-Roussillon

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Les Filets 2024 Piquepoul Blanc (Picpoul de Pinet)

Apricot and nectarine aromas pick up hints of beeswax and yellow rose on the rather complex nose of this bottling. The palate has a bit more heft than others in this category, presenting stonefruit, citrus and coconut flavors. Best Buy. 92 Points — M.K.

$15 Total Wine & More

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Brilliance! Shatterproof Tritan Fluted Recycled Outdoor Wine Glasses Set of 6 Clear

In Stock | $59.95

Shop Now

South America’s Less-Famous Whites

“This summer, I’m going beyond Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc to explore South America‘s diverse white wines, including Sémillon, Torrontes, white blends, and Albariño. I’m starting with the fresh yet layered Bodega Garzón 2024 Petit Clos Albariño from Uruguay’s coastal Maldonado region.”  – Writer-at-Large Jesica Vargas, who reviews wines from Latin American and Africa.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Bodega Garzón 2024 Petit Clos Albariño (Maldonado)

Uruguayan Albariño continues to impress year after year. From Maldonado, near the Atlantic coast, this white wine shows complexity, depth, and refreshing acidity, balancing a combination of delicious flavors. Flowers, honey, and sweet pear on the nose give way to white fruit, ginger, and subtle herbal notes on the lively palate. Editor’s Choice. 94 Points — J.V.

Inquire with winery

Dry Riesling from the Finger Lakes

“After the coldest, snowiest winter in recent memory here in New York, summer could not come sooner. While I crack open white wine any time of the year, my consumption of it probably triples in the hotter months. Dry Riesling plays an especially prominent role on my back deck table thanks to its vivid, thirst-quenching acidity and food friendliness. I have my eye on one from a new(ish) producer on Seneca Lake in the Finger Lakes (my birthplace!) called Ria’s. Their 2024 ‘Viva’ is an aromatic, lemongrass-and-lime blossom strewn bottling with the variety’s hallmark line of racy acidity and lovely slippery texture that ticks all my summer-sipping boxes.” — Writer-at-Large Christina Pickard, who reviews wines from Australia, New Zealand, England, and New York

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Ria’s Wines 2024 Viva Riesling (Seneca Lake)

From an exciting new husband-and-wife team, this lakeside Riesling offers delicate aromas of lemongrass, green tea and lime blossom that become more bombastic with time aerating in glass. In the mouth, it’s a lovely combo of racy lemon pith acidity and slippery textural weight. Still young, this could evolve quite beautifully and complexly over time. Editor’s Choice. 95 Points — C.P.

$30 WineDeals.com

Chillable Reds

“While white wine gets lots of attention during the summer, I really crave low-alcohol, chillable reds during the warmer months. Beaujolais checks all the boxes with bright, fresh acidity while not sacrificing structure. Just give it a quick chill in the fridge for 30 minutes, or put it in a bucket of ice like you would a white wine or rosé and give it time to warm up a bit in your glass.” —Writer-at-Large Reggie Solomon, who reviews wines from France, Spain, and other regions

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Château des Jacques 2022 Morgon

This wine engages and enlivens the senses. Rhubarb and cherry-cola aromas begin on the nose followed by hints of dried-cranberry skin and forest floor. Tannins are refined and soulful on the palate. Pair with chicken paillard, skirt steak and mushroom risotto. 92 Points — R.S.

$27 Wine.com

White Blends from Lebanon

“I’m a big fan of drinking bottles that feel special on otherwise unremarkable days. Few wines bring the oh-hey-now-it’s-an-occasion vibes quite like this 2018 white blend from Lebanon’s iconic Château Musar. Serve it with an at-home mezze spread featuring peak summer produce or a Costco chicken; either way, you won’t be disappointed.” — Writer-at-Large Emily Saladino, who reviews wines from Croatia, Georgia, Greece, Hungary, and Lebanon.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Château Musar 2018 White Blend (Bekaa Valley)

The beautifully textured palate of this creamy, amber-hued wine has nuanced lemon, tangerine, clove, honey, and gently oxidative flavors. Expect velvety tannins on the long, citrus-driven finish. 96 Points — E.S.

title: 30 Summer Cocktails to Help Beat the Heat
summary:
From Mai Tais and Micheladas to mocktails and margaritas, these refreshing summer sippers will keep you cool all season long.

The post 30 Summer Cocktails to Help Beat the Heat appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
If there’s one thing we can all agree on, it is that summer is prime time for enjoying mixed drinks al fresco. When it’s 75 degrees outside and the sun stays high in the sky late into the evening, indulging in a bright summer cocktail (or mocktail) just feels right. So what is the best warm season sipper? With so many good options, the answer to that question is much less obvious. 

Of course, there are the classics. The Aperol spritz is light and refreshing with just enough syrupy bitterness. The Piña Colada—refreshingly sweet and boozy both frozen and on the rocks—is practically synonymous with balmy weather. 

If you want to mix it up beyond the tried and true, options abound. Shake up a spicy mezcalita—the Tajín rim is not optional—for the perfect blend of salty, smoky, spicy, and citrusy flavors. Or, combine red wine and cola to create the aperitif-appropriate Spanish Kalimotxo. 

Going alcohol free this summer? We’ve got that covered too with non-alcoholic spirit replacements and mocktails that are non-alcoholic by design. 

No matter what you choose, these 30 downright delicious cocktails are certain to cool you down and quench your thirst all season long.

Tequila and Mezcal: The Unofficial Summer Spirits

Classic Margarita

It’s generally accepted that the iconic margarita evolved from the Daisy cocktail. The jury is still out on the original creator, this margarita combining blanco tequila, orange liquor, and lime juice is as classic and complete as they come.

Mezcalita

OK, fine—Tajín is technically optional, but we highly recommend it here (and always). When combined with smoky mezcal and tart lime juice, the salty-spicy rim takes the mezcalita to the next level. 

Ranch Water

This Texas summertime staple uses three ingredients—tequila, lime, and sparkling water—for a refreshing, easy cocktail that basically goes down like water.

Mezcal Negroni

Smoky and bitter, the mezcal Negroni works any time of year. But the addition of agave somehow makes it better suited for warm weather than the OG. In our opinion, it undeniably tastes best when sipped on the sidewalk in denim cutoffs and flip flops, as the sun sets and a feel-good soundtrack plays in the background.

Paloma

This bubbly, grapefruit-forward Paloma is all about simplicity. Combining tequila, grapefruit juice, and soda water, it works just as well at brunch time as it does on the beach with your toes in the sand.

Tequila Sunrise

Orange juice, grenadine, and blanco tequila are the stars in the sweet Tequila Sunrise.

The Guavabonga

Packed with pineapple, guava, and orange, this zesty mezcal cocktail is a refreshing foil to the summer heat. 

In the Shop

Upcycled Wine Bottle Highball Glasses (Set of 6) – Clear

In Stock | $59.99

Shop Now

Spritzes, Wine Cocktails, and Everything in Between

Aperol Spritz

We could wax poetic about the Aperol spritz’s many merits forever. Perfectly balanced and supremely refreshing, from brunch to beach to bar, this drink suits any and every warm weather occasion.

Hugo Spritz

Similar to the Aperol spritz but with sweet elderflower flair, Hugo spritz is a refreshing, low-alcohol sparkling wine cocktail with a gentle, floral flavor and a hint of herbaceous mint.

Sangria

Sangria variations are truly endless, but you can’t go wrong with a classic Tempranillo sangria. This batched recipe features a full bottle with pomegranate juice, lemon-lime soda, brandy, and the traditional fruit.

Limoncello Spritz

Looking for a new refreshing, low-abv spritz? We’ve got you covered. The limoncello spritz is simple, fruity, and delicious.

White Wine Spritzer

This fizzy wine and soda cocktail has been an iconic summertime symbol since the ’70s. Throw in a few jalapeño slices with Sauvignon Blanc or some frozen peaches for a modern twist.

Bellini

Forget those canned juice cocktails from bottomless brunch. The traditional Bellini recipe leans on fresh peaches for a sensational sparkling mix.

Kalimotxo

The Kalimotxo, also known as the Calimocho, is beloved across Spain. Made with a 3:1 ratio of red wine and cola, it’s about time the rest of the world embraces this simple and delicious summer slammer.

Michelada

The michelada is a choose-your-own-adventure beer cocktail. While the Mexican lager base is non-negotiable, you can tweak the recipe from there to make it as salty, citrusy, or spicy as you please.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Vienna Break-Resistant Cabernet Sauvignon Wine Glass-Set of 4

In Stock | $100

Shop Now

All Things Tropical and Tiki

Piña Colada

Popularized in the 1960s and ‘70s, the Piña Colada blends tart pineapple and creamy coconut for a tropical-style cocktail perfect for any day in the sun.

Rum Punch

A single serving of this spicy and fruity rum punch might seem contradictory. But fear not! This recipe easily scales up to punch bowl size, and doing so will make you the most popular person at any party.

Modern Strawberry Daiquiri

The classic poolside strawberry daiquiri gets a glow-up in this modern twist. The spicy-smokey flavors of mezcal and Fernet elegantly elevate with the classic strawberry and lime base.

Mai Tai

A tiki mainstay, the Mai Tai combines two types of rum with citrus-forward ingredients and nutty orgeat syrup. No matter where you are, one sip of this tropical classic and you’ll be conjuring images of palm trees and white-sand beaches in seconds.

USS Bomber

Hailing from New York City’s Maison Premiere, this unique cocktail’s combination of coconut milk and blue curacao is totally tiki, while its ombre layers are perfectly patriotic for holiday weekend parties.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Brilliance! Shatterproof Tritan Ribbed Recycled Outdoor Wine Glasses Set of 6 Assorted Color

In Stock | $59.95

Shop Now

Refreshing and Fruit-Forward

Pimm’s Cup

The classic Pimm’s Cup is a 1:2 ratio of Pimm’s No. 1 and sparkling English lemonade. The cucumber, apple, strawberry, and mint garnishes are essential. 

Salty Dog

A mix of grapefruit juice and gin or vodka, the Salty Dog is served over ice with a salted rim. This puckery, saline, cooling beverage might just be the perfect highball variation to beat the summer heat.

Blackberry Bourbon Smash

Few ingredients can brighten up bourbon like blackberries and mint. This fresh and fruity cocktail proves, yet again, that whiskey can go down well year-round.

Mojito

Tainted by years of overly-muddled sugar bombs and sordid reputations among bartenders, the classic lime, mint, and rum mojito is shockingly simple and refreshing when made properly. 

Watermelon Mojito

This watermelon mojito features fresh fruit juice and vibrant ginger beer for mojito lovers who want a little more refreshing complexity. Need we say more?

Gin Daisy

A predecessor to the margarita, this gin-spiked sipper is reminiscent of the now-ubiquitous agave cocktail—minus the tequila.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Brilliance! Shatterproof Tritan Ribbed Recycled Stemless Wine Glasses (Set of 6) – Assorted Colors

In Stock | $49.95

Shop Now

No Alcohol Doesn’t Mean No Fun

Non-Alcoholic Smoky Paloma

With a smoke-treated glass and homemade sage simple syrup, this non-alcoholic smoky Paloma riff is guaranteed to impress. 

The Sober Spritz

The Sober Spritz combines a non-alcoholic aperitif with elderflower syrup and sparkling water for a summery NA riff on the herbal and floral Hugo Spritz. 

The Detox

An intriguing combination of pineapple and celery, the Detox comes together with a savory dash of sea salt.

Non-Alcoholic Margarita

Fresh citrus and non-alcoholic tequila keep this virgin margarita recipe balanced.

title: Gavi
summary:
A Guide to Gavi Gavi DOCG is an Italian white wine region with impressive range and a strong sense of place. In this hilly corner of southeastern Piedmont, the Cortese grape finds its defining expression in vineyards shaped by altitude, exposure, and three distinct soil zones. The denomination’s moderate continental climate, marked by cold winters,

The post Gavi appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
A Guide to Gavi

(Gah·vee)

Gavi DOCG is an Italian white wine region with impressive range and a strong sense of place. In this hilly corner of southeastern Piedmont, the Cortese grape finds its defining expression in vineyards shaped by altitude, exposure, and three distinct soil zones. The denomination’s moderate continental climate, marked by cold winters, hot summers, and breezes that blow inland from the Ligurian coast, helps give Gavi its hallmark freshness, finesse, and complexity, whether the wines are enjoyed young and floral or with more age and mineral depth.

Spread across roughly 1,600 hectares and 11 municipalities in the province of Alessandria, Gavi is both a wine appellation and destination. It’s a region with deep roots, with evidence of grape cultivation dating back to the Middle Ages, finally earning DOCG status in 1998. Today, that long-established wine culture is matched by a modern focus on quality and stewardship, from detailed vineyard mapping by altitude, exposure, and sunlight, to sustainability efforts that include environmental bio-monitoring with bees. And these impressive factors are only some of the reasons why Gavi remains one of Italy’s benchmark white wines.

By the Numbers

Vineyard Area: 1,600 hectares

Municipalities: 11 – Bosio, Carrosio, Capriata d’Orba, Francavilla Bisio, Gavi, Novi Ligure, Parodi Ligure, Pasturana, San Cristoforo, Serravalle Scrivia, and Tassarolo

DOCG Certification: 1998

Grape: 100% Cortese

Annual Production: About 14 million bottles

Distribution: Export to 100 countries

Grape Variety

Gavi wine is made from 100% Cortese, an indigenous Piedmontese white grape that has deep roots in this territory, tended by farmers from more than 250 local families. It is a vigorous varietal that requires careful pruning, minimal tillage, and selective thinning to ensure low yields and preserve quality.  

In the glass, Cortese shows bright citrus, white flowers, fresh fruit, and a signature bitter almond note. But the grape’s biggest selling point is its versatility. It can be zippy and aperitivo-ready, balanced and food friendly, or rich and complex with time in the bottle.

If Barolo and Barbaresco are Piedmont’s marquee reds, Gavi provides the perfect foil as the signature white. It has heritage, identity, and range, as well as a contemporary appeal that’s timeless in the glass and at the table.

History

The history of Gavi wine and its denomination is rooted in a long-standing viticultural tradition that dates back over a thousand years. 

Positioned along the ancient frontier between the sea and the plains, Gavi has been a strategic political and commercial foothold in the region for millennia. Dating back to the second century BC, the Roman city of Libarna was already an important trading hub. Located in present-day Serravalle Scrivia, it sat at the crossroads of three key Roman trade routes: the Via Postumia, Via Francigena, and the Salt Road. 

Nearly 1,500 years later, starting in the Middle Ages, the area developed under Genoese influence. The landscape was shaped by feudal estates, vineyards, forests, waterways, and early production sites such as mills, ironworks, and sawmills.

The earliest documented evidence of vine cultivation in Gavi can be traced to 972, under the authority of the Archbishop of Genoa.

By the late 18th century, Gavi wines had begun to gain recognition beyond local borders, with documented exports departing from the port of Genoa to the New World. 

The first dedicated plantings of the Corteste grape were established in 1856, followed by formal ampelographic studies that highlighted its suitability for high-quality wine production. By the early 20th century, Gavi became synonymous with the Cortese grape—essentially defining the region’s signature style of white wines.

In more recent decades, efforts have focused on quality and preservation. In 1998, Gavi was officially awarded DOCG status—the highest designation in the Italian wine system—defining its production area across the 11 municipalities.

Local Lore

Legend has it, the name Gavi comes from Princess Gavia, the fictional daughter of the real-life Frankish King Clodomer who made the region her home back in the 6th century. There are different versions of how and why Gavia ended up in the rolling hills of the area that would eventually bear her name. Some accounts claim she sought refuge from her enemies. Others say it was a case of forbidden love. She fell for a soldier her father didn’t approve of, so they ran away to a remote village in northwestern Italy.

No matter the case (or historical accuracy), this saga became intertwined with the identity of the territory, symbolising resilience, protection, and a deep connection between people and place. And the romantic myth continues to echo through the vineyards and landscapes of Gavi to this day.

This Region Is Tasted
by Jeff Porter

Jeff Porter reviews wines from Northern Italy (Piedmont, Lombardy, Liguria, Veneto, Alto Adige, Trentino, Friuli-Venezia Giulia), Central Italy (Emilia-Romagna, Marche & Umbria), Slovenia, and Switzerland. A renowned sommelier, Porter’s career began in Napa Valley, before joining acclaimed Osteria Mozza in Los Angeles, followed by Del Posto Ristorante in NYC, and eventually taking the helm as beverage operations director for Bastianich Hospitality Group. Today, he creates beverage lists for a roster of clients around the world and reviews thousands of bottles a year for Wine Enthusiast.

Find a Tasting Room Near Gavi

Coppo

WineryVia Alba 68, Canelli, IT GaviView Website Make a Reservation
&

The Municipalities of Gavi DOCG

Soils and Styles

Gavi is generally perceived as fresh, easy-drinking white wine, appreciated for its immediacy and vibrancy. Yet, with time in bottle, Gavi sheds its youthful simplicity for complexity and depth. Tertiary notes of honey, dried fruit, and more pronounced mineral nuances emerge as the structure broadens, acidity integrates, and wine evolves into a rich, ageworthy wine that deserves a spot in the cellar.

Northern Belt
In the northern part of Gavi, red clay soils rich in iron from alluvial deposits help produce wines with more body and structure. This is the warmest part of the denomination, and the wines here often show a little more breadth and weight.

Central Strip
This belt is made up of alternating marl and sandstone, along with clays, sands, and pebbles. This mix yields wines with a compelling balance of flavor, texture, and distinct freshness.

Southern Belt
In the southernmost part of Gavi where the vineyards increase in elevation and the Apennines draw closer, pale marine marls result in wines defined by more delicate, fine-boned expressions with marked lift and minerality.

Explore Gavi DOCG

This section is brought to you by Gavi DOCG.

A Study in Terroir

Gavi is by no means a one-note wine category. It is not defined by styles, but by soils. Here, the Cortese grape becomes a lens through which the land speaks—subtle, precise, and deeply expressive.

White soils, pale marls of ancient marine origin, are characterized by alternating layers of marl and sandstone. A mix of clays, sands, and pebbles can be found in fluvial terraces, marine formations, and rocks from the oceanic crust. This is where Gavi wines with great balance between structure and flavor come from.

Move to the red soils, richer in clay and iron, and the expression shifts. The wines gain breadth and warmth, offering riper fruit, a more generous mouthfeel, and a deeper structure. This is the area with the warmest climate, yielding Gavi wines with excellent body and structure.

Gavi is more than a fresh white wine: every bottle reveals a denomination rooted in nuance, where identity is shaped underground long before it reaches the glass.

A Versatile Partner for Pairing

Its most distinctive pairing is with the Ligurian-Piedmontese cuisine of this area: it works beautifully with ravioli, trofie al pesto, and classic Piedmontese dishes, from vitello tonnato to tajarin with truffle.

Yet Gavi’s true strength lies in its versatility. Beyond traditional Italian cuisine—seafood, vegetables, pasta, and fried dishes—it pairs effortlessly with a wide range of global flavors. From the precision and umami-driven profiles of Japanese cuisine to the aromatic spices of Middle Eastern dishes, to the bold contrasts of American fusion and the vibrant, fermented notes of Korean cooking, Gavi adapts with ease.

Its precision is key: it cleanses the palate, complements the dish, and never overpowers. In more structured and evolved expressions, it can also support more complex and layered preparations.

Not Just a Wine, But a Destination

More than just a wine, Gavi is a destination. Rolling hills shift in color and composition, revealing a landscape shaped by centuries of geologic and human history. Located just one hour and a half from Milan and Turin, and within easy reach of Genoa and the Ligurian coast, Gavi is a natural crossroads of culture and landscape. It is also just about an hour from Portofino and close to the renowned Serravalle Designer Outlet, making it an ideal destination that blends wine, leisure, and lifestyle.

For lovers of arts and culture, the Forte di Gavi and the archaeological site of Libarna are essential stops. Libarna stands as one of the most significant Roman sites in Northern Italy: an ancient city where traces of theaters, roads, and residential areas still tell the story of a vibrant commercial hub deeply connected to agriculture and wine production. Walking through its remains offers a tangible link to a past where Gavi was already a land of exchange, culture, and viticulture.

Sports and nature lovers can enjoy excursions on foot, horseback, or mountain bikes along trails that venture into the Apennine. From the top of these rollercoaster-like paths, the peak of Monte Tobbio (1092 m) offers stunning panoramas of the Ligurian Sea and the Gulf of Genoa. 

Here, every hill has its own distinct voice—and so does every glass.

title: These Zippy Sauvignon Blancs Are Crushing It
summary:
Zippy bottles from New Zealand, floral Sancerres, and more made our list of highly drinkable Sauvignon Blancs.

The post These Zippy Sauvignon Blancs Are Crushing It appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Bright, crisp, and zesty, there are few wines more refreshing than Sauvignon Blanc. (Though these Chardonnays are looking pretty delicious right now, too!) Hailing from France, this white grape is now planted all over the world, producing popular wines from its homeland as well as New Zealand, Italy, the United States, and beyond.

As Sauvignon Blanc’s location changes, so does its flavor profile. While French versions can shine with floral, grassy notes, Sauvy Bs from other parts of the world can veer more fruit-forward and even tropical.

Though it wasn’t easy, we narrowed down our list of the best bottles to 15—each one showcasing the grape’s diverse skillset.

New Zealand

Few places have made a grape variety their calling card the way New Zealand—and especially the region of Marlborough—have with Sauvignon Blanc.

Christina Pickard, who reviews New Zealand bottlings for Wine Enthusiast, puts it plainly. “Marlborough Sauvignon Blancs are never short on fruit,” she says. “In fact, that pop of citrus, tropical, and passionfruit—along with green herbal and summer vegetable garden characters—is what makes this wine style so recognizable and so wildly popular across the globe.”

But there’s far more variety here than the reputation for zingy freshness might suggest. More and more producers are opting to ferment their Sauvignon Blanc in oak barrels (a distinctly French strategy) representing “an increasingly common departure from the more widespread crisp-and-clean stainless steel ferment,” Pickard explains.

Meanwhile, other producers are employing wild yeast and extended lees contact to “coax out the variety’s salty, flinty minerality while still celebrating its inherently bright fruit and zippy freshness,” she notes.

Clos Henri 2024 Otira Glacial Stones Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)

There’s a gentleness to this single site Sauvignon, from a top French-owned biodynamic estate. It’s concentrated, flavorsome, but never forced or over-made. There’s pronounced minerality—like stones, shells and fresh cut meadow crunching underfoot—and salted margarita vibes, with pineapple rind garnish. Bright, tangy acidity and herb-and-citrus flavors aren’t shy on the palate, but they never shout their presence. Mouth-watering and textural enough to be food friendly; the mineral core anchors a ship of flavor. Editor’s Choice. 95 Points — Christina Pickard

$23 Saratoga Wine

Greywacke 2023 Wild Sauvignon Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)

Anything with “wild” in the name indicates a wine that walks to its own beat, which Greywacke’s now iconic wine certainly does. No green veg-and-fruit combo typically found in Marlborough’s famed Sauvy B style here. Instead, a great swathe of rich, reductive characters appear from first inhale: toasted cashews, matchstick and grilled lemons. It’s waxy, salty and evocative. The palate is all about texture, rich and creamy but never heavy, thanks to permeating acidity that keeps the mouth watering. Sip beside grilled mushrooms or asparagus risotto. 95 Points — C.P.

$25 Total Wine & More

Te Mata 2023 Cape Crest Sauvignon Blanc (Hawke’s Bay)

Made with a touch of Semillon and Sauvignon Gris, this oak-aged Sauvy B is made more in the white Bordeaux vein than the Marlborough one. The nose shows flinty, toasty reductive notes. But the winemaking technique can’t fully obscure SB’s bombastic nature. There’s lemon-lime, pink grapefruit and pineapple with seashells, fennel fronds and summer herbs on the fringes; lovely richness and concentration on the palate, with salted grapefruit acidity and structure to make this a food friendly drop now, but one with the potential to age for several more years. 93 Points — C.P.

$38 Zachys

California

California’s expansive wine regions offer a tapestry of Sauvignon Blanc styles, reflecting the Golden State’s varied climates and winemaking philosophies. From the foggy valleys of Mendocino to the sun-drenched vineyards of Napa, Sauvignon Blanc can thrive in California’s diverse terroirs.

“There is a thumbprint being shown region by region in California,” says Tonya Pitts, Wine Enthusiast writer at large and Californian wine reviewer. “It’s great to see the range of style and flavor profile vary by region.”

That regional character shines through in the selections below, with each bottle telling a slightly different story.

Saracina 2022 Lolonis Vineyard Sauvignon Blanc (Mendocino)

Aromatics of crisp citrus, juicy melon, and dried herbs cascade across the nose. The mouthfeel is fresh with high energy, saline, and mouth-watering lime blossoms, White grapefruit, and fennel, creating a need to experience the concentrated bounty of this luminous nod to Summer. The Lolonis Vineyard is a prized site that has been my favorite for many years. It is a captivating expression of old vine Sauvignon Blanc. Pair with steamed mussels, garlic aioli. 96 Points — Tonya Pitts

$43 Flatiron Wines & Spirits

The Setting Wines 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (Sonoma County)

This richer-styled Sauvignon Blanc delivers focused aromas of ripe melon, candied ginger, pithy citrus and a whisper of flint on the nose, giving way to decadent flavors of grapefruit, honeydew, white flowers and chalky minerality on the palate before a lifted, acid-driven finish with echoes of orange blossom and sunshine. Editor’s Choice. 94 Points — Tom R. Capo

$55 The Setting

Larkmead 2023 Lillie Sauvignon Blanc (Napa Valley)

Succulent and fresh simultaneously, this 100% Sauvignon Blanc lifts from the glass with aromas of honeysuckle and citrus blossom, carrying into flavors of citrus pith, and spices. Mineral freshness completes the wine. 93 Points —Elaine Chukan Brown

$75 Larkmead

Italy

The foothills of the Alps produce some of the most unexpectedly stunning Sauvignon Blancs in the world. In Italy’s Alto Adige region, specifically, elevation and diurnal shifts give wines a clarity that’s both refreshing and powerful. 

Jeff Porter, Wine Enthusiast’s Northern Italian wine reviewer, sums up what makes these bottlings so great: “These wines are driven by their mountain extremes—warm days and cool nights—so ample aromas and flavors but with vibrant acidity and stony minerality.”

There’s less of the herbal edge typical of other regions, and more of a citrus-bright profile accented by alpine herbs. “Their palate tends to be broad but with electric acidity, so the wine fills the mouth but then zips like lightning,” Porter adds.

Cantina Terlano 2023 Quarz Sauvignon Blanc (Alto Adige Terlano)

If you think you know Sauvignon Blanc, this wine might flip your script. It’s got that classic grapefruit and herbs thing going on, but then it goes somewhere entirely different. There’s this wild smoky note that feels like early morning fog rolling over stone fruit orchards. The texture is what really gets you though – it’s got weight but floats across your palate like morning mist. Not trying to be Loire or New Zealand, just confidently doing its own Alto Adige thing. Pure mountain energy in a bottle. Drink Now – 2045. Cellar Selection. 95 Points — Jeff Porter

$90 B21

Luigino Balozio 2019 Sauvignon Cuar Sauvignon Blanc (Friuli Colli Orientali)

A kaleidoscope of aromas flows from this glass of this unique and delicious Sauvignon Blanc. Aromas of salted limes, sage, white flowers, sweet corn and green melon swirl about, a captivating nose, that is hard to put down. Tart and vigorous on the palate with a zippy energy that brightens that palate as green herbs, key lime and lemon all lead down to a path of sea salt and crushed stones on the vibrant finish. Buy a few and put at least one away, you will be pleasantly surprised by how well it will age. 93 Points — J.P.

$44 Liquor Express

Tenuta San Leonardo 2024 Vette Sauvignon Blanc (Vigneti delle Dolomiti)

Like hiking through an alpine meadow with a basket of citrus. The freshness hits different here, more crystalline than crisp. This is the wine equivalent of that perfect summer day when everything just feels right. Grilled shrimp would make it even better. 91 Points — J.P.

$26 Zachys

Bordeaux

While Bordeaux is perhaps best known for its reds, its whites—especially those from Pessac-Léognan and select parts of the Graves—offer some of the world’s most elegant expressions of Sauvignon Blanc. Often blended with Sémillon and aged in oak, these wines have the ability to show texture and gravitas.

There are bottles whose status and cost reflect that gravitas, but the famed region actually offers incredible diversity. “The range is wide, from wines under $15 to some sold for more than $200, so there is plenty of choice,” says Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large and Bordelais Wine Reviewer Roger Voss. “From crisp, young wines, dominated by herbal and citrus flavors to serious wood-aged wine that will last for many years.”

Château Margaux 2022 Pavillon Blanc de Château Margaux (Bordeaux Blanc)

This is a dense wine with richness and a tropical fruit aroma. Comes from the earliest harvest ever for this estate’s white grapes. There is a tantalizing grapefruit edge that shows the essence of the wine’s Sauvignon Blanc. Drink from 2026. 94 Points — Roger Voss

$420 Wine.com

Château la Louvière 2023 Pessac-Léognan

Pure, crisp Sauvignon Blanc, this wine shows a fine balance between herbal and tropical fruit flavors. Its quince and honey aroma and vivid grapefruit flavor are fresh and deliciously fruity. Drink from 2026. 92 Points — R.V.

$39 Millesima

Chateau Langlet 2024 Graves

A ripe, full wine with a fine balance between acidity and ripe tropical flavors. A mown grass aroma and a tight texture give the wine a fresh edge and aging potential. Drink from 2026. Editors’ Choice. 90 Points — R.V.

$26 Wine.com

Sancerre

“Sancerre is the paragon to which all Sauvignon Blanc on the planet is inevitably compared,” says Reggie Solomon, Wine Enthusiast writer at large and reviewer of wines from the Loire Valley.

While traditionally associated with grassy and herbal notes, Solomon points out that these wines have “become fuller and more robust in the last decade while still retaining attractive acidity and minerality.”

The best bottles show Sauvignon Blanc’s potential for elegance and complexity. Some see lees aging and even subtle oak, elevating gently ripened fruit to a new level.

And while Sancerre dominates wine lists, Solomon reminds us not to sleep on neighbors like Menetou-Salon and Pouilly-Fumé, which “offer value with similar depth of flavor” and definitely deserve attention.

Maison de Madeleine 2023 Sancerre Caillottes Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre)

This gorgeous Sancerre is a chameleon in a glass whose character flips on the palate while remaining entirely delicious. Intense golden-fruit aromas of baked yellow apple and ripe Anjou pear surrender to brioche, bread dough, toast, bruised honeysuckle blossom and orange zest on the nose. The palate is creamy and delicious with yeasty elements bending a knee to ripe orchard fruit that asserts its hegemony while acknowledging its depth-inducing sidekick.  94 Points — Reggie Solomon

$33 The Wine Rack

Domaine Gérard Fiou 2023 Le Grand Roc Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre)

This Sancerre is gorgeously complex. Gently-layered aromas of orange zest, ripe golden apple, dried mushroom and dew-dusted forest floor entice the nose that sees added notes of hay. Pure, elegant fruit is perfectly balanced with a supportive layer of acidity with well-integrated flavors that shine in the glass. Editor’s Choice. 94 Points — R.S.

$40 Sokolin

More White Wine Coverage

  • We found 5 Italian white wines that are fantastic for aging.
  • Looking for a crisp white wine? We explain what “crisp” actually means. 
  • Zippy, high-acid Albariño is having a year.

title: How to Serve Your Wine at the Right Temperature This Summer
summary:
From reds to whites and rosés, here's a complete guide to finding the right wine temp on the hottest of days.

The post How to Serve Your Wine at the Right Temperature This Summer appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Hitting the right wine serving temperature is actually a lot harder than it seems. Even some restaurants—where wine is ostensibly someone’s job—can struggle to hit the right service temp, sending out reds that have been baking in a warm “cellar” or whites so cold they feel like drinking hard seltzer. 

At home, many of us are working with whatever wine storage setup the kitchen counter and fridge can offer. Sure, it does the trick, but during the high heat of the summer months, chances are you probably want whatever you’re drinking to err on the side of chilled, at least to some degree. 

Don’t stress: we’re here to help with your summer wine service needs. Whether you’re drinking reds, whites, or rosés, we’ve compiled a guide to finding the right wine temp on the hottest of days.

Red Wine

The dusty old rule of serving reds at “room temperature” is somewhat misleading in the modern day. “That guideline goes back to European homes of the 18th and 19th centuries, which didn’t have good insulation or central heating,” says Fred Swan, professor of wine studies at Delta College. “60˚ F was common room temperature. Today, most people keep their homes from the mid-60s to the low-70s. That’s way too warm for any wine.” 

He also points to rising alcohol levels in today’s red wines, which he notes turn “boozy and gooey” when they’re served too warm. So, no matter the season, chances are your red wines are being served way too warm.

The answer isn’t to suddenly serve all red wines chilled, but to think in terms of weight and structure. A good rule of thumb is the lighter, the cooler. Fresh, low-tannin reds like Gamay, Frappato, or delicate Pinot Noir can benefit from a bit more chill. Think around 55–60˚ F, which is cool enough to lift the freshness without flattening the wine. A short fridge stint—say, around 20 minutes—is usually enough to get a lighter-bodied red into the sweet spot. 

Plusher reds like Cabernet, Syrah, and full-bodied blends can take a bit of cooling, but not so much that tannins become aggressive and complexity gets muted. Ideally, these should be served at 60–65˚ F. During the summer months, maybe even a touch cooler since they will lose their chill more quickly. If your house runs on the warmer side, a brief stay of 10 to 15 minutes in the fridge can help bring some vibrancy back to a heavier style red. 

“It’s not about serving reds warm or cool,” says Serge Doré, founder and president of import company Serge Doré Selections. “It’s about protecting their balance.”

Marshall Tilden III, president of Wine Express & Education at Wine Enthusiast, recommends starting any bottle a little bit cooler than you think you need to, since wines warm up much faster in the heat. It’s also worth keeping the bottle in a chiller and opting for more frequent, lighter pours to keep things at a nice temperature for every sip. 

IN THE SHOP

Double-Walled Iceless Wine Bottle Chiller

In Stock | $24.95

Shop Now

“If you don’t have a wine cellar or wine fridge set to a specific temperature, your best bet is to pop it in the fridge for a little bit,” says Autumn Gilliam, wine educator and owner of The Adventurous Sip. She notes that even a quick 15-minute spell can make a meaningful difference. 

IN THE SHOP

Wine Enthusiast 50-Bottle Wine Refrigerator

In Stock | $599

Shop Now

Tilden has a 20/20 rule: “If red wine is sitting out in the open, you want to put it in the fridge for 20 minutes. If white wine is in the fridge, you want to take it out 20 minutes before serving.”

White Wine

White wines usually end up at the other end of the spectrum. “People probably serve most whites and rosés too cold, pouring straight out of the refrigerator,” says Swan. “That’s going to hide a lot of the fruit and may also make oaked wines seem bitter.” 

Your kitchen fridge typically runs pretty cold, around 35–38˚ F, for food safety reasons. A calibrated wine fridge is noticeably warmer—about 45–55˚ F. So wines pulled from next to the ketchup bottle may be refreshing but not exactly interesting.

As with reds, style also matters here. “A crisp Sauvignon Blanc can be served quite cool to emphasize freshness,” says Doré. “But a richer white like a Meursault or a white Rhône will show far better a few degrees warmer, where texture, depth, and oak integration can emerge.”

Tilden suggests that anything below the low 40s is too cold for most still whites, but adds a caveat for the summer months: “You may need to chill it down to the high 30s so that by the time it gets in your glass and that outside warm air hits the wine, you can enjoy it at the perfect serving temp.” 

If you need to chill a wine quickly, a 50/50 ice-and-water bath works faster and gives you more control. From room temperature, that can take as little as 10 minutes for a red and about 20 minutes for a white. If you’re aiming for even colder—say, a brisk high 30s with the idea that the wine will warm up quickly once outside in the heat—plan on closer to 20 to 30 minutes. 

IN THE SHOP

Coolin Curve Wine and Champagne Chiller Bucket

In Stock | $40

Shop Now

Rosé

There’s a reason rosé gets its own season: it’s just that quaffable, especially in warm weather. But it’s also the wine most likely to get overcorrected in the name of crushability. 

“If you put [rosé] in an ice bucket for 30 minutes or more, you will have completely suffocated it,” says Doré. “The aromatics will be muted, textures lighten, and the wine loses its ability to express nuance. What you’re left with is freshness—but without personality.”

Gilliam suggests 45–55˚ F for rosé. From room temperature, it should take rosé about 20 minutes in the fridge or 10 to 15 minutes in an ice-and-water bath. If your bottle is coming straight from a cold kitchen fridge, let it sit out for 10 to 20 minutes before serving. This should ensure it stays crisp and refreshing without dulling the fruit and aromatics.  

Style still plays a role, but even that is a suggestion, not a commandment. “I can’t say that I have seen these wines being overchilled as a trend, but if you enjoy it cooler than standard on a hot summer day, then go for it,” says Tilden. “However you enjoy it is the right serving temp.”

It’s a thought that bears repeating: there is no wrong way to drink wine you enjoy. Swan notes that even dropping an ice cube into a glass is more acceptable than some would have you believe. “I’ve seen plenty of Europeans do that during summer lunches al fresco. And I have it from reliable sources that Jacques Pépin and Robert Mondavi have done it on occasion,” he says. “The important thing is enjoying the wine and the experience.” 

IN THE SHOP

Wine Enthusiast Brilliance! Shatterproof Tritan Fluted Recycled Stemless Wine Glasses (Set of 6)

In Stock | $49.95

Shop Now

If you do want to take some of the guesswork out on a more permanent basis, investing in a dedicated wine storage solution is worth considering. “For everyday wine drinkers, a standard wine fridge is great,” notes Gilliam. “Keep it set at 55˚ F and everything will be close to proper temperature.” 

For households that regularly keep all types of wine on hand, Swan recommends a dual-zone cooler with one zone set at 50–52˚ F for lighter whites and chillable reds, and another at 55–57˚ F for fuller whites and bigger reds. Not a requirement, of course, but it’ll help you get the right temp for every bottle—no extra chilling necessary.

IN THE SHOP

Wine Enthusiast VinoView L 145 Smart Wi-Fi Dual Zone Wine Cellar

In Stock | $2,289

Shop Now

title: Take the Kids on Your Next Wine Trip. Seriously.
summary:
As wineries rethink their approach to hospitality, wine country is becoming the family destination you never expected.

The post Take the Kids on Your Next Wine Trip. Seriously. appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
This year, my 21-year-old son and I went to our first wine festival together: the Rhode Island Wine Experience. Granted, he’s old enough now to understand the basics of wine regions and flavor profiles, but as we tasted from winemakers around the world, we got plenty of comments about how cool it was for a mother and son to bond over wine.

“How did you get him so interested?” people asked me in amazement.

My family’s love for wine goes way back. I’ve been taking my three kids (and nieces and nephews) on vineyard trips for years. Until recently, wine country was largely seen as a sophisticated, adult-only escape, but today many wineries have begun opening their doors to children with play areas, access to farm animals, and casual food options. After all, little ones bring parents, and that means more families can embrace wine country travel without feeling left out.  

Given that the wine industry has spent the past few years grappling with ways to include more people, this shift in attitude makes sense.

In the United States, per-capita wine consumption has been falling since its 2017 peak, according to IWSR, with Gen Z drinking less alcohol than generations preceding them. American tasting rooms, in particular, have suffered from dwindling interest. Against that backdrop, the calculus of welcoming families to wineries has shifted into a new style of hospitality.

‘Return to what wine country has always been’ 

At some estates, the presence of children is organic. “We don’t see it as a long-term strategy so much as a return to what wine country has always been,” says Dan Zepponi, president and CEO of Cuvaison in Napa Valley. The property, which features two open-air boathouse pavilions overlooking the winery’s pond, also includes The VineYard, a family-friendly outdoor zone designed for relaxed, self-paced tastings, with lots of room to move and vineyard views.

“When people experience wine as part of time spent together, across generations, around food, and near nature, it feels authentic and special,” says Zepponi.  Rather than emphasizing transactions (i.e. bottle sales), these wineries are leaning into a memory-making model.

At Demetria Estate in the Santa Ynez Valley, catering to families just made sense. The biodynamic property features sweeping lawns, resident dogs, and horseback riding through the hills above Santa Barbara wine country. Owner Alexis Zahoudanis says hospitality runs through her veins.  

“Demetria has always been a family-owned and operated estate, rooted in my father’s Greek heritage and my mother’s Southern hospitality,” she says. “When they founded Demetria two decades ago, their vision was to create a place where guests could relax, connect with nature, and experience an authentic, lived-in wine estate.” She says the family-friendly activities evolved organically as guests responded favorably to that sense of home. 

The result is a destination where a serious wine tasting and a child’s afternoon of adventure can coexist. Parents are unhurried because their kids are genuinely occupied, and the wine still gets the attention it deserves.

That dynamic is something Karen DeGolia, proprietor of Limerick Lane Cellars in Healdsburg, knows well. Her estate holiday homes welcome multi-generational groups regularly, and the thank-you notes she receives afterward tend to focus less on the wine than on the experiences.  

“They tell us about the special memories they created in the vineyards, in the pool, and at game time in the family room,” she says. When parents want time alone or a romantic dinner in town, grandparents take the children. When everyone gathers, the century-old vines are right there to explore. 

“This part of the country is so beautiful, and there are so many things to do,” DeGolia adds. “We love welcoming guests from all over the world to our little corner of the Russian River Valley.” (Healdsburg was recently named the Friendliest Town in America by WorldAtlas, a designation that feels fitting for a wine region increasingly oriented toward hospitality.)

After years of chasing younger drinkers through creative bottlings and branding, the wine industry may find that its most durable answer is not in the product but in the place. The most thoughtful estates understand that the child running through the vines today is not a distraction from the wine business. They are the wine business, twenty-something years from now. 

However, Zepponi says the goal is not to convert children into wine consumers, but to let them absorb wine as something woven into life and as a way that families can mark time together. “Those moments naturally leave a lasting impression,” he says. 

Even one afternoon of wine tasting with loved ones can evolve into a lifelong memory. My son lives thousands of miles away from me these days, but shared wine experiences make that distance feel a lot smaller. It’s proof that making room for kids on the vineyard can have lasting benefits, for families and winemakers.

title: Moscato d’Asti Is the Moment
summary:
Consorzio dell'Asti DOCG director Giacomo Pondini lets us in on why Moscato d'Asti is so much more than a "sweet wine."

The post Moscato d’Asti Is the Moment appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
This episode of the Wine Enthusiast Podcast is brought to you by the Consorzio dell’Asti DOCG.

Something is shifting in the way people drink wine. Nowadays, it feels less about chasing power or prestige and more about finding something that fits the moment. In that context, Moscato d’Asti is landing in a very interesting place.

For years, the wine was pigeonholed as simply sweet and fizzy. But that shorthand never told the full story. Today, as more people look for wines that are lighter, more flexible, and easier to enjoy, Moscato d’Asti starts to make a lot more sense. With alcohol levels often hovering around 5 percent, it delivers flavor, freshness, and lift without asking too much from the drinker.

In this conversation, Giacomo Pondini, director of Asti DOCG, pushes back on the idea that Moscato d’Asti needs to change. The better question is: Have we been looking at it the right way? The wine is about balance and precision, and that has only become more important as producers work through the realities of a warmer climate while trying to protect its signature vibrancy.

Moscato d’Asti is also a wine that deserves to be rethought at the table. Moving past the dessert-only labeling opens things up—pairing with spice, salt, heat, and even umami-driven dishes can bring out a completely different side of the wine. It becomes less about sweetness and more about energy and contrast.

There is also movement within the denomination itself. The arrival of Asti Rosé shows a willingness to explore new expressions while staying rooted in aromatic identity.

Moscato d’Asti has always had range. But right now, more people seem ready to see it.

On the basics of Moscato d’Asti

Giacomo Pondini: “Moscato d’Asti is not just a sweet wine—it’s a wine that is balanced, aromatic with a light sparkling, where the sweetness is not just the only component, it’s one of the components, because all these components in the end balance the outcome of the wine that you’re tasting. There’s a vibrant acidity with light effervescence that always enhances freshness. And the 5% usually that you find in Moscato d’Asti grape, 5% of alcohol, makes the wine so airy, not heavy. It’s always the right time to have a glass of Moscato d’Asti.”

On how Moscato d’Asti gets its signature effervescence

GP: “The fizziness comes from a specific way of producing this wine that actually was invented in the late 19th century by Federico Martinotti, a local oenologist that was helping wineries to produce a wine, a sparkling wine, that didn’t have to go through the two different moments of the classic method. Asti was born as a sparkling wine around 1865 in the classic method, but by the end of that century, there was the introduction of a new way of producing wine that was created by Federico Martinotti…. He helped in producing wine with the second fermentation directly in the tank. That was allowing producers to avoid all the losses of bottles that they were facing at the beginning of production, and that also allowed them to control the fermentation and stop the fermentation at a specific moment where they wanted a specific level of sugar, but most of all, when they wanted to have a lower alcohol percentage between five and six, which is the level of alcohol in Moscato d’Asti nowadays as well.”

On Moscato d’Asti’s Italian origins

GP: “It comes from the agricultural tradition. So that means that it became a wine seen by the Italian consumers, mainly, as a perfect match for desserts or sweet treats. But the thing is that, being born in the country, it was used by people who were living in the country, and they were pairing it—they probably didn’t even have enough money to buy the cake on Sunday, or maybe they could produce it at home, but they used that Moscato d’Asti to boost their day-to-day life, using it on their normal meals. It’s a traditional match/pairing here to use Moscato d’Asti with the so-called “merenda sinoira”— that moment, that brunch that you have when you come back home from working in the fields, and you need to boost yourself…. They used to eat bread, butter, and anchovies, and a glass of Moscato d’Asti that matches perfectly. The saltiness of the anchovies, the butteriness of the butter, the sweet tendency of the bread.”

On convincing consumers that it’s more than just a sweet wine

GP: “Well, it’s quite easy, because first of all, you just have to taste it, and then you realize that the wine perfectly matches every kind of pairing that you have in your mind. But at the same time, you can also have some more technical insight, saying that the sweetness of the wine is always balanced by a great acidity, a great freshness that doesn’t make the wine too heavy for your palate. And the sweetness that you may feel in that glass usually matches perfectly [with] food and dishes that are modern, that are spicy, that are hot. And you always search for something with more residual sugar when you have, for example, when you have a Chinese hotpot in front of you, or you have a Thai soup with that light spiciness that matches the sweetness of the Moscato. So we really like that. Moscato d’Asti is a contemporary wine that can match hundreds of food dishes from all over the world.”

On the appeal to low-alcohol-focused consumers

GP: “Before low-alcohol became a trend, Moscato d’Asti was already there. We’ve always been there, naturally, because this wine is not de-alcoholized. This wine has always been having the same level of alcohol because it comes from a tradition of winemaking that highlights the great characteristics of an aromatic grape called Moscato, and this wine reflects perfectly the taste of the berry of this grape. That means that you have an immediate connection to the berry, to the grapes, to the food that you eat. And so, in terms of representing the variety we will eat, Moscato d’Asti is the ideal choice to understand the character of a Moscato grape.”

On Moscato d’Asti’s ideal food pairing

Jeff Porter: “I know, for me, that one of the most interesting, kind of, savory pairings, and you alluded to it before, but like… Thai, Vietnamese, Southeast Asian cuisine. Because I think Moscato d’Asti, when I break it down in my head, as a sommelier and as a journalist—there’s the fruit component, which for me is normally peach, apricot, and nectarine. Then there’s an herbal component, which could be mint or then also maybe bay leaf, or something like sage, but definitely on that green spectrum. And then you’ve got just the vibrant, electric acidity that balances that sweetness.

But again, like when you get a really, really good spicy chili on your palate, and then you hit it, you get that herbaceousness, the fire from the chili. And this could be in Mexican cuisine, it could be in Thai. It could be in Calabrian cuisine. And then you get that splash of Moscato d’Asti, it just really sets everything right, where your palate just feels at one. I think that’s so special about food and wine pairing, and I think people have to look outside… beyond dessert with this wine.”

On recent developments in Asti DOCG

GP: “Well, the Asti DOCG recently made a change in the code of production, allowing also the producers of sparkling method to introduce a rosé, a style of sparkling Asti. Because we also introduced the use of Brachetto grapes, which are aromatic, which is an aromatic red variety, local red variety, that, just like Moscato, has a great history here in Piedmont. It’s always been together with Moscato—one of the traditional grapes always used in local productions—and we wanted to highlight this aromatic profile that makes these wines different from all the others. And we believe that also with that, with this new style of wine, we can be even more interesting for new generations, for consumers, for those who are looking for something low in alcohol, but really high in pleasure. So we believe that we are giving an answer to the market that is looking for something new, something… although there are many sparkling rosé wines, we believe that being aromatic, being made based on Brachetto and Moscato, this wine is going to have success on the market, on many markets all over the world.”

The Guest 

Giacomo Pondini is the director of the Consorzio dell’Asti DOCG. He has previously held the position of director at both the Consorzio del Vino Brunello di Montalcino and the Consorzio Tutela Morellino di Scansano.

More About the Podcast

The Wine Enthusiast Podcast is your serving of drinks culture and the people who drive it. You can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. Visit the podcast homepage for more episodes and transcripts.

More Podcast Episodes

  • Oregon’s sparkling wines are stunning—does it have what it takes to be the next Champagne?
  • What’s next for the wine importer who took down Trump’s tariffs?
  • Ashley Graham upended the fashion world. Next up? Wine.
  • In defense of the undersung bottles that line grocery store shelves.
  • After 10 Grammy wins, Norah Jones’ pivot to wine is hitting the right notes.
